**Norway**,[[a]](#cite_note-20) officially the **Kingdom of Norway**,[[b]](#cite_note-21) is a [Nordic country](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries") comprising the western and northernmost parts of the [Scandinavian Peninsula](/wiki/Scandinavian_Peninsula "Scandinavian Peninsula") in [Northern Europe](/wiki/Northern_Europe "Northern Europe"), the remote [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic "Arctic") island [Jan Mayen](/wiki/Jan_Mayen "Jan Mayen") and the [archipelago](/wiki/Archipelago "Archipelago") [Svalbard](/wiki/Svalbard "Svalbard").[[note 5]](#cite_note-Svalbard-23) [Bouvet Island](/wiki/Bouvet_Island "Bouvet Island"), located in the [Subantarctic](/wiki/Subantarctic "Subantarctic"), is a [dependency](/wiki/Dependencies_of_Norway "Dependencies of Norway"), and not a part of the Kingdom; Norway also [claims](/wiki/Territorial_claims_in_Antarctica "Territorial claims in Antarctica") the Antarctic territories of [Peter I Island](/wiki/Peter_I_Island "Peter I Island") and [Queen Maud Land](/wiki/Queen_Maud_Land "Queen Maud Land"). Norway has a population of approximately 5.6 million, and a total area of 385,207 square kilometres (148,729 sq mi).[[10]](#cite_note-kart_2019-14) Its capital and largest city is [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo"). The country shares a long eastern border with [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), and is bordered by [Finland](/wiki/Finland "Finland") and [Russia](/wiki/Russia "Russia") to the northeast. Norway has an extensive coastline facing the [Skagerrak](/wiki/Skagerrak "Skagerrak") strait, the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"), the [Norwegian Sea](/wiki/Norwegian_Sea "Norwegian Sea"), and the [Barents Sea](/wiki/Barents_Sea "Barents Sea").

The unified kingdom of Norway was established in 872 as a merger of [petty kingdoms](/wiki/Petty_kingdoms_of_Norway "Petty kingdoms of Norway") and has existed continuously for 1,153–1,154 years. From 1537 to 1814, Norway was part of [Denmark–Norway](/wiki/Denmark%E2%80%93Norway "Denmark–Norway"), and, from 1814 to 1905, it was in [a personal union](/wiki/Union_between_Sweden_and_Norway "Union between Sweden and Norway") with Sweden. Norway was neutral during the [First World War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), and in the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II") until April 1940 when it [was invaded](/wiki/Operation_Weser%C3%BCbung "Operation Weserübung") and [occupied](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Norway "German occupation of Norway") by [Nazi Germany](/wiki/Nazi_Germany "Nazi Germany") until the end of the war.

[Harald V](/wiki/Harald_V "Harald V") of the [House of Glücksburg](/wiki/House_of_Gl%C3%BCcksburg "House of Glücksburg") is the current [King of Norway](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Norway "Monarchy of Norway"). [Jonas Gahr Støre](/wiki/Jonas_Gahr_St%C3%B8re "Jonas Gahr Støre") has been [Prime Minister of Norway](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Norway "Prime Minister of Norway") since 2021. As a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state") with a [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy"), Norway [divides state power](/wiki/Separation_of_powers "Separation of powers") between the [parliament](/wiki/Storting "Storting"), the [cabinet](/wiki/Council_of_State_(Norway) "Council of State (Norway)"), and the [judiciary](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Norway "Judiciary of Norway"), as determined by the [1814 constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Norway "Constitution of Norway"). Norway has both administrative and political subdivisions on two levels: [counties](/wiki/Counties_of_Norway "Counties of Norway") and [municipalities](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_of_Norway "List of municipalities of Norway"). The [Sámi people](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_peoples "Sámi peoples") have a certain amount of self-determination and influence over traditional territories through the [Sámi Parliament](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_Parliament_of_Norway "Sámi Parliament of Norway") and the [Finnmark Act](/wiki/Finnmark_Act "Finnmark Act"). Norway [maintains close ties](/wiki/Norway%E2%80%93European_Union_relations "Norway–European Union relations") with the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union") and the [United States](/wiki/Norway%E2%80%93United_States_relations "Norway–United States relations"). Norway is a founding member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations"), [NATO](/wiki/NATO "NATO"), the [European Free Trade Association](/wiki/European_Free_Trade_Association "European Free Trade Association"), the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe"), the [Antarctic Treaty](/wiki/Antarctic_Treaty_System "Antarctic Treaty System"), and the [Nordic Council](/wiki/Nordic_Council "Nordic Council"); a member of the [European Economic Area](/wiki/European_Economic_Area "European Economic Area"), the [WTO](/wiki/World_Trade_Organization "World Trade Organization"), and the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD"); and a part of the [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area"). The Norwegian dialects form a [continuum](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum") of [mutual intelligibility](/wiki/Mutual_intelligibility "Mutual intelligibility") with [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language") and [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language") dialects.

Norway maintains the [Nordic model](/wiki/Nordic_model "Nordic model") with [universal health care](/wiki/Universal_health_care "Universal health care") and a comprehensive [social security](/wiki/Welfare_spending "Welfare spending") system, with its values rooted in egalitarian ideals.[[17]](#cite_note-24) The Norwegian state has large ownership positions in key industrial sectors, having extensive reserves of [petroleum](/wiki/Petroleum "Petroleum"), [natural gas](/wiki/Natural_gas "Natural gas"), minerals, [lumber](/wiki/Lumber "Lumber"), [seafood](/wiki/Seafood "Seafood"), and fresh water. The [petroleum industry](/wiki/Petroleum_industry "Petroleum industry") accounts for around a quarter of the country's [gross domestic product](/wiki/Gross_domestic_product "Gross domestic product") (GDP).[[18]](#cite_note-25) On a [per-capita](/wiki/Per_capita "Per capita") basis, Norway is the world's largest producer of oil and natural gas outside of the Middle East.[[19]](#cite_note-26)[[20]](#cite_note-27) The country has the [fourth- and eighth-highest](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (PPP) per capita") PPP-adjusted per-capita GDP in the world on the [World Bank](/wiki/World_Bank "World Bank")'s and [IMF](/wiki/International_Monetary_Fund "International Monetary Fund")'s list, respectively.[[21]](#cite_note-28) It has the world's largest [sovereign wealth fund](/wiki/Government_Pension_Fund_of_Norway "Government Pension Fund of Norway"), with a value of US$2.1 trillion.[[22]](#cite_note-29)

## Etymology

Main article: [Etymology of Norway](/wiki/Etymology_of_Norway "Etymology of Norway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/10/Ohthere.jpg/500px-Ohthere.jpg)](/wiki/File:Ohthere.jpg)

Opening of [Ohthere](/wiki/Ohthere_of_H%C3%A5logaland "Ohthere of Hålogaland")'s [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") account, translated: "Ohthere told his lord *Ælfrede* king that he lived northmost of all Norwegians…"

Norway has two official names: *Norge* in [Bokmål](/wiki/Bokm%C3%A5l "Bokmål") and *Noreg* in [Nynorsk](/wiki/Nynorsk "Nynorsk"). The English name Norway comes from the [Old English](/wiki/Old_English "Old English") word *Norþweg* in the account provided by [Ohthere of Hålogaland](/wiki/Ohthere_of_H%C3%A5logaland "Ohthere of Hålogaland") around 890, meaning "northern way" or "way leading to the north", which is how the [Anglo-Saxons](/wiki/Anglo-Saxons "Anglo-Saxons") referred to the coastline of Atlantic Norway.[[23]](#cite_note-Nomino-NRK-30)[[24]](#cite_note-Tvil-Forskning-31)[[25]](#cite_note-32) The Anglo-Saxons of Britain also referred to Norway in 890 as *Norðmanna land*.[[23]](#cite_note-Nomino-NRK-30)[[24]](#cite_note-Tvil-Forskning-31)

There is some disagreement about whether the native name of Norway originally had the same etymology as the English form. According to the traditional dominant view, the first component was originally [*norðr*](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/nor%C3%B0r "wikt:norðr"), a [cognate](/wiki/Cognate "Cognate") of English *north*, so the full name was *Norðr [vegr](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/vegr "wikt:vegr")*, "the way northwards", referring to the sailing route along the Norwegian coast, and contrasting with *suðrvegar* "southern way" (from [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse") [suðr](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/su%C3%B0r "wikt:suðr")) for (Germany), and *austrvegr* "eastern way" (from [austr](https://en.wiktionary.org/wiki/austr "wikt:austr")) for the [Baltic](/wiki/Baltic_Sea "Baltic Sea").[[26]](#cite_note-33)

## History

Main articles: [History of Norway](/wiki/History_of_Norway "History of Norway") and [History of Scandinavia](/wiki/History_of_Scandinavia "History of Scandinavia")

### Prehistory

Main article: [Scandinavian prehistory](/wiki/Scandinavian_prehistory "Scandinavian prehistory")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c9/Bronze_Age_boats.png/250px-Bronze_Age_boats.png)](/wiki/File:Bronze_Age_boats.png)

[Nordic Bronze Age](/wiki/Nordic_Bronze_Age "Nordic Bronze Age") [rock carvings](/wiki/Rock_carvings "Rock carvings") at [Steinkjer](/wiki/Steinkjer "Steinkjer"), [Central Norway](/wiki/Central_Norway "Central Norway")

The earliest traces of human occupation in Norway are found along the coast, where the huge ice shelf of the [last ice age](/wiki/Last_glacial_period "Last glacial period") first melted between 11,000 and 8000 BC. The oldest finds are stone tools dating from 9500 to 6000 BC, discovered in [Finnmark](/wiki/Finnmark "Finnmark") ([Komsa culture](/wiki/Komsa_culture "Komsa culture")) in the north and [Rogaland](/wiki/Rogaland "Rogaland") ([Fosna culture](/wiki/Fosna-Hensbacka_culture "Fosna-Hensbacka culture")) in the southwest. Theories about the two cultures being separate were deemed obsolete in the 1970s.[[27]](#cite_note-34)

Between 3000 and 2500 BC, new settlers ([Corded Ware culture](/wiki/Corded_Ware_culture "Corded Ware culture")) arrived in [eastern Norway](/wiki/Eastern_Norway "Eastern Norway"). They were [Indo-European](/wiki/Proto-Indo-Europeans "Proto-Indo-Europeans") farmers who grew grain and kept livestock, and gradually replaced the hunting-fishing population of the west coast.

### Metal Ages

Main articles: [Nordic Bronze Age](/wiki/Nordic_Bronze_Age "Nordic Bronze Age") and [Iron Age Scandinavia](/wiki/Iron_Age_Scandinavia "Iron Age Scandinavia")

From about 1500 BC, [bronze](/wiki/Bronze "Bronze") was gradually introduced. Burial cairns built close to the sea as far north as [Harstad](/wiki/Harstad_Municipality "Harstad Municipality") and also inland in the south are characteristic of this period, with rock carving motifs that differ from those of the [Stone Age](/wiki/Stone_Age "Stone Age"), depicting ships resembling the [Hjortspring boat](/wiki/Hjortspring_boat "Hjortspring boat"), while large stone burial monuments known as [stone ships](/wiki/Stone_ship "Stone ship") were also erected.[[28]](#cite_note-35)

There is little archaeological evidence dating to the early [Iron Age](/wiki/Iron_Age "Iron Age"), which were the last 500 years BC. The dead were cremated, and their graves contained little goods. During the first four centuries AD, the people of Norway were in contact with Roman-occupied [Gaul](/wiki/Gaul "Gaul"); about 70 Roman bronze cauldrons, often used as burial urns, have been found. Contact with countries farther south brought a knowledge of [runes](/wiki/Runes "Runes"); the oldest known Norwegian runic inscription dates from the third century.[[29]](#cite_note-36)

### Viking Age

Main articles: [Petty kingdoms of Norway](/wiki/Petty_kingdoms_of_Norway "Petty kingdoms of Norway") and [Viking Age](/wiki/Viking_Age "Viking Age")

See also: [Unification of Norway](/wiki/Unification_of_Norway "Unification of Norway") and [Hereditary Kingdom of Norway](/wiki/Hereditary_Kingdom_of_Norway "Hereditary Kingdom of Norway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9c/Le_bateau_viking_dOseberg_%284835828216%29.jpg/250px-Le_bateau_viking_dOseberg_%284835828216%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Le_bateau_viking_dOseberg_(4835828216).jpg)

The [Oseberg ship](/wiki/Oseberg_ship "Oseberg ship") at the [Viking Ship Museum](/wiki/Viking_Ship_Museum_(Oslo) "Viking Ship Museum (Oslo)") in Oslo

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/d5/Viking_swords_at_Bergen_Museum.jpg/250px-Viking_swords_at_Bergen_Museum.jpg)](/wiki/File:Viking_swords_at_Bergen_Museum.jpg)

[Viking swords](/wiki/Viking_sword "Viking sword") found in Norway, preserved at the [Bergen Museum](/wiki/Bergen_Museum "Bergen Museum")

By the time of the first historical records of Scandinavia, about the 8th century, several small political entities existed in Norway. It has been estimated that there were nine petty realms in Western Norway during the early [Viking Age](/wiki/Viking_Age "Viking Age").[[30]](#cite_note-37) Archaeologist Bergljot Solberg on this basis estimates that there would have been at least 20 in the whole country.[[31]](#cite_note-38)

In the Viking period, Norwegian Viking explorers discovered [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland") by accident in the ninth century when heading for the [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands "Faroe Islands"), and eventually came across [Vinland](/wiki/Vinland "Vinland"), known today as [Newfoundland](/wiki/Newfoundland_(island) "Newfoundland (island)"), in Canada. The Vikings from Norway were most active in the northern and western [British Isles](/wiki/British_Isles "British Isles") and eastern [North America isles](/wiki/Norse_colonization_of_North_America "Norse colonization of North America").[[32]](#cite_note-39)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Gjermundbu_helmet_-_cropped.jpg/250px-Gjermundbu_helmet_-_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gjermundbu_helmet_-_cropped.jpg)

The [Gjermundbu helmet](/wiki/Gjermundbu_helmet "Gjermundbu helmet") found in [Buskerud](/wiki/Buskerud "Buskerud") is the only known reconstructable [Viking Age](/wiki/Viking_Age "Viking Age") helmet.

According to tradition, [Harald Fairhair](/wiki/Harald_Fairhair "Harald Fairhair") unified them into one in 872 after the [Battle of Hafrsfjord](/wiki/Battle_of_Hafrsfjord "Battle of Hafrsfjord") in [Stavanger](/wiki/Stavanger "Stavanger"), thus becoming the first king of a united Norway.[[33]](#cite_note-40) Harald's realm was mainly a South Norwegian coastal state. Fairhair ruled with a strong hand and according to the sagas, many Norwegians left the country to live in Iceland, the [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands "Faroe Islands"), [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland"), and parts of [Britain](/wiki/Great_Britain "Great Britain") and Ireland.[[34]](#cite_note-41)

[Haakon I the Good](/wiki/Haakon_I_of_Norway "Haakon I of Norway") was Norway's first Christian king, in the mid-10th century, though his attempt to introduce the religion was rejected. [Norse traditions](/wiki/Norse_mythology "Norse mythology") were replaced slowly by [Christian ones](/wiki/Christian_mythology "Christian mythology") in the late 10th and early 11th centuries. This is largely attributed to the missionary kings [Olaf I Tryggvasson](/wiki/Olaf_I_of_Norway "Olaf I of Norway") and [Olaf II Haraldsson](/wiki/Olaf_II_of_Norway "Olaf II of Norway") (St. Olaf). Olaf Tryggvasson conducted raids in England, including attacking London. Arriving back in Norway in 995, Olaf landed in [Moster](/wiki/Moster_(island) "Moster (island)") where he built a church which became the first [Christian church](/wiki/Old_Moster_Church "Old Moster Church") in Norway. From Moster, Olaf sailed north to [Trondheim](/wiki/Trondheim_(city) "Trondheim (city)") where he was proclaimed King of Norway by the Eyrathing in 995.[[35]](#cite_note-42) One of the most important sources for the history of the 11th century Vikings is the treaty between the Icelanders and Olaf II Haraldsson, king of Norway circa 1015 to 1028.[[36]](#cite_note-43)

[Feudalism](/wiki/Feudalism "Feudalism") never really developed in Norway or Sweden, as it did in the rest of Europe. However, the administration of government took on a very conservative feudal character. The [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") forced royalty to cede to them greater and greater concessions over foreign trade and the economy, because of the loans the Hansa had made to the royals and the large debt the kings were carrying. The League's monopolistic control over the economy of Norway put pressure on all classes, especially the peasantry, to the degree that no real [burgher](/wiki/Burgher_(social_class) "Burgher (social class)") class existed in Norway.[[37]](#cite_note-44)

### High Middle Ages

Main article: [Kingdom of Norway (872–1397)](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Norway_(872%E2%80%931397) "Kingdom of Norway (872–1397)")

See also: [Greater Norway](/wiki/Greater_Norway "Greater Norway") and [Civil war era in Norway](/wiki/Civil_war_era_in_Norway "Civil war era in Norway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ec/Norwegian_Hereditary_Empire_excluding_Greenland.png/250px-Norwegian_Hereditary_Empire_excluding_Greenland.png)](/wiki/File:Norwegian_Hereditary_Empire_excluding_Greenland.png)

[The Norwegian Kingdom](/wiki/Kingdom_of_Norway_(872%E2%80%931397) "Kingdom of Norway (872–1397)") at its greatest extent during the 13th century, including the [Open Border](/wiki/Norway%E2%80%93Russia_border#History "Norway–Russia border") with the Novgorod Republic

From the 1040s to 1130, the country was at peace.[[38]](#cite_note-45) In 1130, the [civil war era](/wiki/Civil_war_era_in_Norway "Civil war era in Norway") broke out on the basis of [unclear succession laws](/wiki/Line_of_succession_to_the_Norwegian_throne "Line of succession to the Norwegian throne"), which allowed the king's sons to rule jointly. The [Archdiocese of Nidaros](/wiki/Archdiocese_of_Nidaros "Archdiocese of Nidaros") was created in 1152 and attempted to control the appointment of kings.[[39]](#cite_note-46) The church inevitably had to take sides in the conflicts. The wars ended in 1217 with the appointment of [Håkon IV Håkonsson](/wiki/H%C3%A5kon_IV "Håkon IV"), who introduced clear laws of succession.[[40]](#cite_note-47)

From 1000 to 1300, the population increased from 150,000 to 400,000, resulting both in more land being cleared and the subdivision of farms. While in the Viking Age farmers owned their own land, by 1300, seventy per cent of the land was owned by the king, the church, or the aristocracy, and about twenty per cent of yields went to these landowners.[[41]](#cite_note-48)

The 13th century saw Norway's [golden age](/wiki/Golden_age_(metaphor) "Golden age (metaphor)"), marked by peace, increased trade with the British Islands, and rising German influence toward the century's end. Throughout the [High Middle Ages](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages"), the king established Norway as a sovereign state with a central administration and local representatives.[[42]](#cite_note-49)

In 1349, the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") spread to Norway and within a year killed a third of the population. Later plagues reduced the population to half the starting point by 1400. Many communities were entirely wiped out, resulting in an abundance of land, allowing farmers to switch to more [animal husbandry](/wiki/Animal_husbandry "Animal husbandry"). The reduction in taxes weakened the king's position,[[43]](#cite_note-50) and many aristocrats lost the basis for their surplus. High [tithes](/wiki/Tithe "Tithe") to church made it increasingly powerful and the archbishop became a member of the [Council of State](/wiki/Norwegian_Council_of_State "Norwegian Council of State").[[44]](#cite_note-s45-51)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a8/Barrio_de_Bryggen%2C_Bergen.jpg/250px-Barrio_de_Bryggen%2C_Bergen.jpg)](/wiki/File:Barrio_de_Bryggen,_Bergen.jpg)

[Bryggen](/wiki/Bryggen "Bryggen") in [Bergen](/wiki/Bergen_(city) "Bergen (city)"), once the centre of trade in Norway under the [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") trade network, now preserved as a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site")

The [Hanseatic League](/wiki/Hanseatic_League "Hanseatic League") took control over Norwegian trade during the 14th century and established a trading centre in [Bergen](/wiki/Bergen_(city) "Bergen (city)"). In 1380, [Olaf Haakonsson](/wiki/Olaf_II_of_Denmark "Olaf II of Denmark") inherited both the Norwegian (as Olaf IV) and Danish thrones (as Olaf II), creating a union between the two countries.[[44]](#cite_note-s45-51) In 1397, under [Margaret I](/wiki/Margaret_I_of_Denmark "Margaret I of Denmark"), the [Kalmar Union](/wiki/Kalmar_Union "Kalmar Union") was created between the three Scandinavian countries. She waged war against the Germans, resulting in a trade blockade and higher taxation on Norwegian goods, which led to [a rebellion](/wiki/Engelbrekt_rebellion "Engelbrekt rebellion"). However, the Norwegian Council of State was too weak to pull out of the union.[[45]](#cite_note-52)

Margaret pursued a centralising policy which inevitably favoured Denmark because of its greater population.[[46]](#cite_note-53) Margaret also granted trade privileges to the Hanseatic merchants of [Lübeck](/wiki/L%C3%BCbeck "Lübeck") in Bergen in return for recognition of her rule, and these hurt the Norwegian economy. The Hanseatic merchants formed a state within a state in Bergen for generations.[[47]](#cite_note-54) The "[Victual Brothers](/wiki/Victual_Brothers "Victual Brothers")" launched three devastating pirate raids on the port (the last in 1427).[[48]](#cite_note-55)

Norway slipped ever more to the background under the [Oldenburg dynasty](/wiki/House_of_Oldenburg "House of Oldenburg") (established 1448). There was one revolt under [Knut Alvsson](/wiki/Knut_Alvsson "Knut Alvsson") in 1502.[[49]](#cite_note-56) Norway took no part in the events which led to Sweden's secession from the Kalmar Union in the 1520s.[[50]](#cite_note-57)

#### Kalmar Union

Main articles: [Kalmar Union](/wiki/Kalmar_Union "Kalmar Union") and [First Swedish–Norwegian union](/wiki/First_Swedish%E2%80%93Norwegian_union "First Swedish–Norwegian union")

Upon the death of King [Haakon V](/wiki/Haakon_V_of_Norway "Haakon V of Norway") in 1319, [Magnus Eriksson](/wiki/Magnus_Eriksson "Magnus Eriksson"), at just three years old, inherited the throne as King Magnus VII. A simultaneous movement to make Magnus King of Sweden proved successful (he was a grandson of King [Magnus Ladulås](/wiki/Magnus_III_of_Sweden "Magnus III of Sweden") of Sweden), and both the kings of Sweden and of Denmark were elected to the throne by their respective nobles. Thus Sweden and Norway were united under King Magnus VII.[[51]](#cite_note-58)

In 1349, the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") killed between 50% and 60% of Norway's population[[52]](#cite_note-59) and led to a period of social and economic decline.[[53]](#cite_note-enc-60) Although the death rate was comparable with the rest of Europe, economic recovery took much longer because of the small, scattered population.[[53]](#cite_note-enc-60) Even before the plague, the population was only about 500,000.[[54]](#cite_note-61) After the plague, many farms lay idle while the population slowly increased.[[53]](#cite_note-enc-60) However, the few surviving farms' tenants found their bargaining positions with their landlords greatly strengthened.[[53]](#cite_note-enc-60)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/17/Union_of_Kalmar_%281500%29.svg/250px-Union_of_Kalmar_%281500%29.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Union_of_Kalmar_(1500).svg)

The [Kalmar Union](/wiki/Kalmar_Union "Kalmar Union"), c. 1500

King Magnus VII ruled Norway until 1350, when his son, Haakon, was placed on the throne as [Haakon VI](/wiki/Haakon_VI_of_Norway "Haakon VI of Norway").[[55]](#cite_note-62) In 1363, Haakon married [Margaret](/wiki/Margaret_I_of_Denmark "Margaret I of Denmark"), daughter of King [Valdemar IV of Denmark](/wiki/Valdemar_IV_of_Denmark "Valdemar IV of Denmark").[[53]](#cite_note-enc-60) Upon the death of Haakon in 1379, his 10-year-old son [Olaf IV](/wiki/Olaf_II_of_Denmark "Olaf II of Denmark") acceded to the throne.[[53]](#cite_note-enc-60) As Olaf had already been elected to the throne of Denmark in 1376,[[53]](#cite_note-enc-60) Denmark and Norway entered a [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union").[[56]](#cite_note-63) Olaf's mother and Haakon's widow, Queen Margaret, managed the foreign affairs of Denmark and Norway during Olaf's minority.[[53]](#cite_note-enc-60)

Margaret was on the verge of achieving a union of Sweden with Denmark and Norway when Olaf IV suddenly died.[[53]](#cite_note-enc-60) Denmark made Margaret temporary ruler on the death of Olaf. On 2 February 1388, Norway followed suit and crowned Margaret.[[53]](#cite_note-enc-60) Queen Margaret knew that her power would be more secure if she were able to find a king to rule in her place. She settled on [Eric of Pomerania](/wiki/Eric_of_Pomerania "Eric of Pomerania"), grandson of her sister. Thus at an all-Scandinavian meeting held at Kalmar, Erik of Pomerania was crowned king of all three Scandinavian countries, bringing the thrones of Norway, Denmark, and Sweden under the control of Queen Margaret when the country entered into the [Kalmar Union](/wiki/Kalmar_Union "Kalmar Union").

### Early modern period

Main article: [Denmark–Norway](/wiki/Denmark%E2%80%93Norway "Denmark–Norway")

After Sweden broke out of the [Kalmar Union](/wiki/Kalmar_Union "Kalmar Union") in 1521, Norway tried to follow suit,[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] but the subsequent rebellion was defeated, and Norway remained in a union with Denmark until 1814. This period was [by some](/wiki/Nicolai_Wergeland "Nicolai Wergeland") referred to as the "400-Year Night", since all of the kingdom's intellectual and administrative power was centred in [Copenhagen](/wiki/Copenhagen "Copenhagen").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Eerste_fase_van_de_Zeeslag_in_de_Sont_-_First_phase_of_the_Battle_of_the_Sound_-_November_8_1658_%28Jan_Abrahamsz_Beerstraten%2C_1660%29.jpg/250px-Eerste_fase_van_de_Zeeslag_in_de_Sont_-_First_phase_of_the_Battle_of_the_Sound_-_November_8_1658_%28Jan_Abrahamsz_Beerstraten%2C_1660%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Eerste_fase_van_de_Zeeslag_in_de_Sont_-_First_phase_of_the_Battle_of_the_Sound_-_November_8_1658_(Jan_Abrahamsz_Beerstraten,_1660).jpg)

The [Battle of the Sound](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Sound "Battle of the Sound") between an allied Dano-Norwegian–[Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") fleet and the Swedish navy, 8 November 1658 (29 October [OS](/wiki/Old_Style "Old Style"))

With the [introduction of Protestantism](/wiki/Reformation_in_Denmark%E2%80%93Norway_and_Holstein "Reformation in Denmark–Norway and Holstein") in 1536, the archbishopric in Trondheim was dissolved; Norway lost its independence and effectually became a colony of Denmark. The Church's income and possessions were instead redirected to the court in Copenhagen. Norway lost the steady stream of pilgrims to the relics of [St. Olav](/wiki/Olaf_II_of_Norway "Olaf II of Norway") at the [Nidaros](/wiki/Nidaros "Nidaros") shrine and, with them, much of the contact with cultural and economic life in the rest of Europe.

Eventually restored as a kingdom (albeit in legislative union with Denmark) in 1661, Norway saw its land area decrease in the 17th century with the loss of the provinces [Båhuslen](/wiki/Bohusl%C3%A4n "Bohuslän"), [Jemtland](/wiki/J%C3%A4mtland "Jämtland"), and [Herjedalen](/wiki/H%C3%A4rjedalen "Härjedalen") to Sweden, as the result of a number of disastrous wars. In the north, its territory was increased by the acquisition of the provinces of [Troms](/wiki/Troms "Troms") and [Finnmark](/wiki/Finnmark "Finnmark"), at the expense of Sweden and Russia.

The [famine of 1695–1696](/wiki/Great_Famine_of_1695%E2%80%931697 "Great Famine of 1695–1697") killed roughly 10% of Norway's population.[[57]](#cite_note-64) The harvest failed in Scandinavia at least nine times between 1740 and 1800, with great loss of life.[[58]](#cite_note-65)

### Later modern period

Main article: [United Kingdoms of Sweden and Norway](/wiki/United_Kingdoms_of_Sweden_and_Norway "United Kingdoms of Sweden and Norway")

See also: [Norwegian protectorate](/wiki/Norway_in_1814 "Norway in 1814") and [Norwegian romantic nationalism](/wiki/Norwegian_romantic_nationalism "Norwegian romantic nationalism")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Eidsvoll_riksraad_1814.jpeg/250px-Eidsvoll_riksraad_1814.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Eidsvoll_riksraad_1814.jpeg)

The 1814 constitutional assembly, painted by [Oscar Wergeland](/wiki/Oscar_Wergeland "Oscar Wergeland")

After Denmark–Norway was attacked by the [United Kingdom](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_Great_Britain_and_Ireland "United Kingdom of Great Britain and Ireland") at the 1807 [Battle of Copenhagen](/wiki/Battle_of_Copenhagen_(1807) "Battle of Copenhagen (1807)"), it entered into an alliance with [Napoleon](/wiki/Napoleon "Napoleon"), with the war leading to dire conditions and mass [starvation](/wiki/Starvation "Starvation") in 1812. As the Danish kingdom was on the losing side in 1814, it was forced by the [Treaty of Kiel](/wiki/Treaty_of_Kiel "Treaty of Kiel") to cede Norway to Sweden, while the old Norwegian provinces of Iceland, Greenland, and the Faroe Islands remained with the Danish crown.[[59]](#cite_note-66) Norway took this opportunity to declare independence, adopted a constitution based on [American](/wiki/United_States_Constitution "United States Constitution") and [French](/wiki/Constitution_of_France "Constitution of France") models, and elected the Crown Prince of Denmark and Norway, [Christian Frederick](/wiki/Christian_VIII_of_Denmark "Christian VIII of Denmark"), as king on 17 May 1814 – celebrated as the [Syttende mai](/wiki/Norwegian_Constitution_Day "Norwegian Constitution Day") (Seventeenth of May) holiday.

Norwegian opposition to the decision to link Norway with Sweden caused the [Norwegian–Swedish War](/wiki/Swedish%E2%80%93Norwegian_War_(1814) "Swedish–Norwegian War (1814)") to break out as Sweden tried to subdue Norway by military means. As Sweden's military was not strong enough to defeat the Norwegian forces outright, and Norway's treasury was not large enough to support a protracted war, and as British and Russian navies blockaded the Norwegian coast,[[60]](#cite_note-67) the belligerents were forced to negotiate the [Convention of Moss](/wiki/Convention_of_Moss "Convention of Moss"). Christian Frederik abdicated the Norwegian throne and authorised the [Parliament of Norway](/wiki/Parliament_of_Norway "Parliament of Norway") to make the necessary constitutional amendments to allow for the [personal union](/wiki/Personal_union "Personal union") that Norway was forced to accept. On 4 November 1814, the Parliament (Storting) elected [Charles XIII of Sweden](/wiki/Charles_XIII_of_Sweden "Charles XIII of Sweden") as king of Norway, thereby establishing the [union with Sweden](/wiki/Union_between_Sweden_and_Norway "Union between Sweden and Norway").[[61]](#cite_note-68) Under this arrangement, Norway kept its liberal constitution and its own independent institutions, though it shared a monarch and foreign policy with Sweden. Following the recession caused by the [Napoleonic Wars](/wiki/Napoleonic_Wars "Napoleonic Wars"), economic development of Norway remained slow until 1830.[[62]](#cite_note-69)

This period also saw the rise of [Norwegian romantic nationalism](/wiki/Norwegian_romantic_nationalism "Norwegian romantic nationalism"), as Norwegians sought to define and express a distinct national character. The movement covered all branches of culture, including literature ([Henrik Wergeland](/wiki/Henrik_Wergeland "Henrik Wergeland"), [Bjørnstjerne Bjørnson](/wiki/Bj%C3%B8rnstjerne_Bj%C3%B8rnson "Bjørnstjerne Bjørnson"), [Peter Christen Asbjørnsen](/wiki/Peter_Christen_Asbj%C3%B8rnsen "Peter Christen Asbjørnsen"), [Jørgen Moe](/wiki/J%C3%B8rgen_Moe "Jørgen Moe")), painting ([Hans Gude](/wiki/Hans_Gude "Hans Gude"), [Adolph Tidemand](/wiki/Adolph_Tidemand "Adolph Tidemand")), music ([Edvard Grieg](/wiki/Edvard_Grieg "Edvard Grieg")), and even language policy, where attempts to define a native written language for Norway led to today's two official written forms for Norwegian: [Bokmål](/wiki/Bokm%C3%A5l "Bokmål") and [Nynorsk](/wiki/Nynorsk "Nynorsk").

[King Charles III John](/wiki/Charles_XIV_John_of_Sweden "Charles XIV John of Sweden") came to the throne of Norway and Sweden in 1818 and reigned to 1844. He protected the constitution and liberties of Norway and Sweden during the age of [Metternich](/wiki/Klemens_Wenzel,_Prince_von_Metternich "Klemens Wenzel, Prince von Metternich").[*[neutrality](/wiki/Wikipedia:Neutral_point_of_view "Wikipedia:Neutral point of view") is [disputed](/wiki/Talk:Norway#Added_WP:NPOV_tag "Talk:Norway")*] As such, he was regarded as a liberal monarch. However, he was ruthless in his use of paid informers, secret police and restrictions on the freedom of the press to put down public movements for reform—especially the Norwegian national independence movement.[[63]](#cite_note-70)

The [Romantic Era](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") that followed the reign of Charles III John brought some significant social and political reforms. In 1854, women won the right to inherit property. In 1863, the last trace of keeping unmarried women in the status of minors was removed. Furthermore, women were eligible for different occupations, particularly the common school teacher.[[64]](#cite_note-71) By mid-century, Norway's democracy was limited; voting was limited to officials, property owners, leaseholders and burghers of incorporated towns.[[65]](#cite_note-72)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/87/Saami_Family_1900.jpg/250px-Saami_Family_1900.jpg)](/wiki/File:Saami_Family_1900.jpg)

A [Sámi](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_people "Sámi people") family in Norway, c. 1900

Norway remained a conservative society. Life in Norway (especially economic life) was "dominated by the aristocracy of professional men who filled most of the important posts in the central government".[[66]](#cite_note-73) There was no strong bourgeois class to demand a breakdown of this aristocratic control.[[67]](#cite_note-Marx_Engels-74) Thus, even while revolution swept over most of the countries of Europe in 1848, Norway was largely unaffected.[[67]](#cite_note-Marx_Engels-74)

[Marcus Thrane](/wiki/Marcus_Thrane "Marcus Thrane") was a [Utopian socialist](/wiki/Utopian_socialism "Utopian socialism") who in 1848 organised a labour society in [Drammen](/wiki/Drammen_(town) "Drammen (town)"). In just a few months, this society had a membership of 500 and was publishing its own newspaper. Within two years, 300 societies had been organised all over Norway, with a total membership of 20,000 drawn from the lower classes of both urban and rural areas.[[68]](#cite_note-75) In the end, the revolt was easily crushed; Thrane was captured and jailed.[[69]](#cite_note-76)

In 1898, all men were granted [universal suffrage](/wiki/Universal_suffrage "Universal suffrage"), followed by all [women](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage") in 1913.

### Dissolution of the union and the First World War

Main articles: [Union dissolution referendum](/wiki/Norwegian_union_dissolution_referendum,_1905 "Norwegian union dissolution referendum, 1905") and [Dissolution of the union between Norway and Sweden](/wiki/Dissolution_of_the_union_between_Norway_and_Sweden "Dissolution of the union between Norway and Sweden")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7b/Folkeavstemningen%2C_13._august_1905%2C_Maridalen_skole%2C_Norge_for_nordmenn-symbol%2C_Oslo_Museum%2C_OB.NW2889.jpg/250px-Folkeavstemningen%2C_13._august_1905%2C_Maridalen_skole%2C_Norge_for_nordmenn-symbol%2C_Oslo_Museum%2C_OB.NW2889.jpg)](/wiki/File:Folkeavstemningen,_13._august_1905,_Maridalen_skole,_Norge_for_nordmenn-symbol,_Oslo_Museum,_OB.NW2889.jpg)

The slogan "Norge for Nordmænd" (Norway for Norwegians) at a School in Oslo on 13 August 1905, a voting place during the referendum on the dissolution of the union with Sweden

[Christian Michelsen](/wiki/Christian_Michelsen "Christian Michelsen"), Prime Minister of Norway from 1905 to 1907, played a central role in the peaceful separation of Norway from Sweden on 7 June 1905. A national referendum confirmed the people's preference for a monarchy over a republic. However, no Norwegian could legitimately claim the throne, since none of Norway's noble families could claim [royal descent](/wiki/Royal_descent "Royal descent").

The government then offered the throne of Norway to Prince Carl of Denmark, a prince of the Dano-German royal [house of Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg-Glücksburg](/wiki/House_of_Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg-Gl%C3%BCcksburg "House of Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg-Glücksburg") and a distant relative of Norway's medieval kings. Following the plebiscite, he was unanimously elected king by the Norwegian [Parliament](/wiki/Parliament_of_Norway "Parliament of Norway"); he took the name [Haakon VII](/wiki/Haakon_VII_of_Norway "Haakon VII of Norway").

Throughout the [First World War](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I"), Norway remained neutral; however, diplomatic pressure from the [British government](/wiki/Government_of_the_United_Kingdom "Government of the United Kingdom") meant that it heavily favoured the [Allies](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_I "Allies of World War I"). During the war, Norway exported fish to both Germany and Britain, until an [ultimatum](/wiki/Ultimatum "Ultimatum") from the British government and [anti-German sentiments](/wiki/Anti-German_sentiment "Anti-German sentiment") as a result of [German submarines](/wiki/U-boat "U-boat") [targeting](/wiki/Unrestricted_submarine_warfare "Unrestricted submarine warfare") Norwegian merchantmen led to a termination of trade with Germany. 436 Norwegian merchantmen were sunk by the *[Kaiserliche Marine](/wiki/Imperial_German_Navy "Imperial German Navy")*, with 1,150 Norwegian sailors killed.[[70]](#cite_note-77)[*[disputed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Disputed_statement "Wikipedia:Disputed statement") – [discuss](/wiki/Talk:Norway "Talk:Norway")*]

### Second World War

See also: [German occupation of Norway](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Norway "German occupation of Norway"), [Reichskommissariat Norwegen](/wiki/Reichskommissariat_Norwegen "Reichskommissariat Norwegen"), and [Quisling regime](/wiki/Quisling_regime "Quisling regime")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/4d/Bombingen_av_Kristiansund%2C_1940%2C_Riksarkivet%2C_Arkivreferanse_PA_1667U2_050.jpg/250px-Bombingen_av_Kristiansund%2C_1940%2C_Riksarkivet%2C_Arkivreferanse_PA_1667U2_050.jpg)](/wiki/File:Bombingen_av_Kristiansund,_1940,_Riksarkivet,_Arkivreferanse_PA_1667U2_050.jpg)

Bombing of [Kristiansund](/wiki/Kristiansund "Kristiansund"). The German invasion resulted in 24 towns being bombed in the spring of 1940.

Norway once more proclaimed its neutrality during the [Second World War](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), but was [invaded by German forces](/wiki/Operation_Weser%C3%BCbung "Operation Weserübung") on 9 April 1940. Although Norway was unprepared for the German surprise attack (see: [Battle of Drøbak Sound](/wiki/Battle_of_Dr%C3%B8bak_Sound "Battle of Drøbak Sound"), [Norwegian campaign](/wiki/Norwegian_campaign "Norwegian campaign"), and Invasion of Norway), military and naval resistance lasted for two months. Norwegian armed forces in the north launched an offensive against the German forces in the [Battles of Narvik](/wiki/Battles_of_Narvik "Battles of Narvik"), but were forced to surrender on 10 June after losing British support which had been diverted to France during the [German invasion of France](/wiki/Battle_of_France "Battle of France").

King Haakon and the Norwegian government escaped to [Rotherhithe](/wiki/Rotherhithe "Rotherhithe") in London. Throughout the war they sent radio speeches and supported clandestine military actions against the Germans. On the day of the invasion, the leader of the small National-Socialist party [Nasjonal Samling](/wiki/Nasjonal_Samling "Nasjonal Samling"), [Vidkun Quisling](/wiki/Vidkun_Quisling "Vidkun Quisling"), tried to seize power, but was forced by the German occupiers to step aside. Real power was wielded by the leader of the German occupation authority, [Josef Terboven](/wiki/Josef_Terboven "Josef Terboven"). Quisling, as *minister president*, later formed a [collaborationist government under German control](/wiki/Quisling_regime "Quisling regime"). Up to 15,000 Norwegians volunteered to fight in German units, including the [Waffen-SS](/wiki/Waffen-SS "Waffen-SS").[[71]](#cite_note-78)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/Albert_Viljam_Hagelin_with_Adolf_Hitler_and_Vidkun_Quisling%2C_13_February_1942.png/250px-Albert_Viljam_Hagelin_with_Adolf_Hitler_and_Vidkun_Quisling%2C_13_February_1942.png)](/wiki/File:Albert_Viljam_Hagelin_with_Adolf_Hitler_and_Vidkun_Quisling,_13_February_1942.png)

[Adolf Hitler](/wiki/Adolf_Hitler "Adolf Hitler") meets the Norwegian Prime Minister [Vidkun Quisling](/wiki/Vidkun_Quisling "Vidkun Quisling") and minister [Albert Viljam Hagelin](/wiki/Albert_Viljam_Hagelin "Albert Viljam Hagelin"), 13 February 1942

Many Norwegians and persons of Norwegian descent joined the Allied forces as well as the [Free Norwegian Forces](/wiki/Free_Norwegian_Forces "Free Norwegian Forces"). In June 1940, a small group had left Norway following their king to Britain. This group included 13 ships, five aircraft, and 500 men from the Royal Norwegian Navy. By the end of the war, the force had grown to 58 ships and 7,500 men in service in the Royal Norwegian Navy, 5 squadrons of aircraft in the newly formed Norwegian Air Force, and land forces including the [Norwegian Independent Company 1](/wiki/Norwegian_Independent_Company_1 "Norwegian Independent Company 1") and 5 Troop as well as No. 10 [Commandos](/wiki/British_Commandos "British Commandos").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

During [German occupation](/wiki/German_occupation_of_Norway "German occupation of Norway"), Norwegians built a [resistance movement](/wiki/Norwegian_resistance_movement "Norwegian resistance movement") which incorporated civil disobedience and armed resistance including the destruction of [Norsk Hydro](/wiki/Norsk_Hydro "Norsk Hydro")'s [heavy water](/wiki/Heavy_water "Heavy water") plant and stockpile of heavy water at [Vemork](/wiki/Vemork "Vemork"), which [crippled the German nuclear programme](/wiki/Norwegian_heavy_water_sabotage "Norwegian heavy water sabotage"). More important to the [Allied](/wiki/Allies_of_World_War_II "Allies of World War II") war effort, however, was the role of the Norwegian [Merchant Marine](/wiki/Merchant_Navy "Merchant Navy"), the fourth-largest merchant marine fleet in the world. It was led by the Norwegian shipping company [Nortraship](/wiki/Nortraship "Nortraship") under the Allies throughout the war and took part in every war operation from the [evacuation of Dunkirk](/wiki/Dunkirk_evacuation "Dunkirk evacuation") to the [Normandy landings](/wiki/Normandy_landings "Normandy landings"). Every December Norway gives a [Christmas tree](/wiki/Christmas_tree "Christmas tree") to the United Kingdom as thanks for the British assistance during the war.[[72]](#cite_note-79)

[Svalbard](/wiki/Svalbard "Svalbard") was not occupied by German troops, but Germany secretly [established a meteorological station](/wiki/Operation_Haudegen "Operation Haudegen") there in 1944.[[73]](#cite_note-80)

### Post–World War II history

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/18/StatfjordA%28Jarvin1982%29.jpg/250px-StatfjordA%28Jarvin1982%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:StatfjordA(Jarvin1982).jpg)

Since the 1970s oil production has helped to expand the Norwegian economy and finance the Norwegian state ([Statfjord oil field](/wiki/Statfjord_oil_field "Statfjord oil field")).

From 1945 to 1962, the [Labour Party](/wiki/Labour_Party_(Norway) "Labour Party (Norway)") held an absolute majority in the parliament. The government, led by prime minister [Einar Gerhardsen](/wiki/Einar_Gerhardsen "Einar Gerhardsen"), embarked on a programme inspired by [Keynesian economics](/wiki/Keynesian_economics "Keynesian economics"), emphasising state financed [industrialisation](/wiki/Industrialisation "Industrialisation") and co-operation between trade unions and [employers' organisations](/wiki/Employers%27_organisation "Employers' organisation"). Many measures of state control of the economy imposed during the war were continued, although the [rationing](/wiki/Rationing "Rationing") of dairy products was lifted in 1949, while price controls and rationing of housing and cars continued until 1960.

The wartime alliance with the United Kingdom and the United States continued in the post-war years. Although pursuing the goal of a socialist economy, the Labour Party distanced itself from the Communists, especially after the Communists' seizure of power in [Czechoslovakia](/wiki/Czechoslovakia "Czechoslovakia") in 1948, and strengthened its foreign policy and defence policy ties with the US. Norway received [Marshall Plan](/wiki/Marshall_Plan "Marshall Plan") aid from the United States starting in 1947, joined the [Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development") (OECD) one year later, and became a founding member of the [North Atlantic Treaty Organization](/wiki/NATO "NATO") (NATO) in 1949.

Oil was discovered at the small Balder field in 1967, but production only began in 1999.[[74]](#cite_note-81) In 1969, the [Phillips Petroleum Company](/wiki/Phillips_Petroleum_Company "Phillips Petroleum Company") discovered petroleum resources at the [Ekofisk](/wiki/Ekofisk_oil_field "Ekofisk oil field") field west of Norway. In 1973, the Norwegian government founded the State oil company, Statoil (now [Equinor](/wiki/Equinor "Equinor")). Oil production did not provide net income until the early 1980s because of the large capital investment required. Around 1975, both the proportion and absolute number of workers in industry peaked. Since then labour-intensive industries and services like factory mass production and shipping have largely been outsourced.

Norway was a founding member of the [European Free Trade Association](/wiki/European_Free_Trade_Association "European Free Trade Association") (EFTA). Norway was twice invited to join the [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"), but ultimately declined after referendums that failed by narrow margins in [1972](/wiki/1972_Norwegian_European_Communities_membership_referendum "1972 Norwegian European Communities membership referendum") and [1994](/wiki/1994_Norwegian_European_Union_membership_referendum "1994 Norwegian European Union membership referendum").[[75]](#cite_note-82)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/56/2011_Norway_attacks_flower_march_2.jpg/250px-2011_Norway_attacks_flower_march_2.jpg)](/wiki/File:2011_Norway_attacks_flower_march_2.jpg)

Town Hall Square in Oslo filled with people with roses mourning the victims of the [Utøya massacre](/wiki/2011_Norway_attacks#Utøya_mass_shooting "2011 Norway attacks") of 22 July 2011.

In 1981, a [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(Norway) "Conservative Party (Norway)") government led by [Kåre Willoch](/wiki/K%C3%A5re_Willoch "Kåre Willoch") replaced the Labour Party with a policy of stimulating the [stagflated economy](/wiki/Stagflation "Stagflation") with tax cuts, economic liberalisation, deregulation of markets, and measures to curb record-high inflation (13.6% in 1981).

Norway's first female prime minister [Gro Harlem Brundtland](/wiki/Gro_Harlem_Brundtland "Gro Harlem Brundtland") of the Labour Party continued many of the reforms, while backing traditional Labour concerns such as [social security](/wiki/Social_security "Social security"), high taxes, the industrialisation of nature, and feminism. By the late 1990s, Norway had paid off its foreign debt and had started accumulating a [sovereign wealth fund](/wiki/Sovereign_wealth_fund "Sovereign wealth fund"). Since the 1990s, a divisive question in politics has been how much of the income from petroleum production the government should spend, and how much it should save.

In 2011, Norway suffered [two terrorist attacks](/wiki/2011_Norway_attacks "2011 Norway attacks") by [Anders Behring Breivik](/wiki/Anders_Behring_Breivik "Anders Behring Breivik") which struck the [government quarter](/wiki/Regjeringskvartalet "Regjeringskvartalet") in Oslo and a summer camp of the Labour party's [youth movement](/wiki/Workers%27_Youth_League_(Norway) "Workers' Youth League (Norway)") at [Utøya](/wiki/Ut%C3%B8ya "Utøya") island, resulting in 77 deaths and 319 wounded.[[76]](#cite_note-83)

[Jens Stoltenberg](/wiki/Jens_Stoltenberg "Jens Stoltenberg") led Norway as prime minister for eight years from 2005 to 2013.[[77]](#cite_note-84) The [2013 Norwegian parliamentary election](/wiki/2013_Norwegian_parliamentary_election "2013 Norwegian parliamentary election") brought a more conservative government to power, with the Conservative Party and the [Progress Party](/wiki/Progress_Party_(Norway) "Progress Party (Norway)") winning 43% of the electorate's votes.[[78]](#cite_note-85) In the [Norwegian parliamentary election 2017](/wiki/2017_Norwegian_parliamentary_election "2017 Norwegian parliamentary election") the centre-right government of Prime Minister [Erna Solberg](/wiki/Erna_Solberg "Erna Solberg") won re-election.[[79]](#cite_note-reuters.com-86) The [2021 Norwegian parliamentary election](/wiki/2021_Norwegian_parliamentary_election "2021 Norwegian parliamentary election") saw a big win for the left-wing opposition in an election fought on climate change, inequality, and oil;[[80]](#cite_note-87) Labour leader [Jonas Gahr Støre](/wiki/Jonas_Gahr_St%C3%B8re "Jonas Gahr Støre") was sworn in as prime minister.[[81]](#cite_note-88)

## Geography

Main articles: [Geography of Norway](/wiki/Geography_of_Norway "Geography of Norway") and [Geology of Norway](/wiki/Geology_of_Norway "Geology of Norway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/fd/Satellite_image_of_Norway_in_February_2003.jpg/250px-Satellite_image_of_Norway_in_February_2003.jpg)](/wiki/File:Satellite_image_of_Norway_in_February_2003.jpg)

A satellite image of continental Norway and nearby countries in winter

Norway's core territory comprises the western and northernmost portion of the [Scandinavian Peninsula](/wiki/Scandinavian_Peninsula "Scandinavian Peninsula"); the remote island of [Jan Mayen](/wiki/Jan_Mayen "Jan Mayen") and the archipelago of [Svalbard](/wiki/Svalbard "Svalbard") are also included.[[note 5]](#cite_note-Svalbard-23) The Antarctic [Peter I Island](/wiki/Peter_I_Island "Peter I Island") and the sub-Antarctic [Bouvet Island](/wiki/Bouvet_Island "Bouvet Island") are [dependent territories](/wiki/Dependencies_of_Norway "Dependencies of Norway") and thus not considered part of the Kingdom. Norway also claims a section of [Antarctica](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica") known as [Queen Maud Land](/wiki/Queen_Maud_Land "Queen Maud Land").[[82]](#cite_note-Norge-89) Norwegian possessions in the North Atlantic, [Faroe Islands](/wiki/Faroe_Islands "Faroe Islands"), [Greenland](/wiki/Greenland "Greenland"), and [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland"), remained Danish when Norway was passed to Sweden at the [Treaty of Kiel](/wiki/Treaty_of_Kiel "Treaty of Kiel").[[83]](#cite_note-90) Norway also comprised [Bohuslän](/wiki/Bohusl%C3%A4n "Bohuslän") until 1658, [Jämtland](/wiki/J%C3%A4mtland "Jämtland") and [Härjedalen](/wiki/H%C3%A4rjedalen "Härjedalen") until 1645,[[82]](#cite_note-Norge-89) [Shetland](/wiki/Shetland "Shetland") and [Orkney](/wiki/Orkney "Orkney") until 1468,[[84]](#cite_note-91) and the [Hebrides](/wiki/Hebrides "Hebrides") and [Isle of Man](/wiki/Isle_of_Man "Isle of Man") until the [Treaty of Perth](/wiki/Treaty_of_Perth "Treaty of Perth") in 1266.[[85]](#cite_note-92)

Norway comprises the western and northernmost part of [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") in Northern Europe,[[86]](#cite_note-SNL-93) between latitudes [57°](/wiki/57th_parallel_north "57th parallel north") and [81° N](/wiki/81st_parallel_north "81st parallel north"), and longitudes [4°](/wiki/4th_meridian_east "4th meridian east") and [32° E](/wiki/32nd_meridian_east "32nd meridian east"). Norway is the northernmost of the [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries") and if Svalbard is included also the easternmost.[[87]](#cite_note-94) Norway includes the northernmost point on the European mainland.[[88]](#cite_note-95) The rugged coastline is broken by huge [fjords](/wiki/Fjord "Fjord") and thousands of islands. The coastal [baseline](/wiki/Baseline_(sea) "Baseline (sea)") is 2,532 kilometres (1,573 mi). The coastline of the mainland including fjords stretches 28,953 kilometres (17,991 mi), when islands are included the coastline has been estimated to 100,915 kilometres (62,706 mi).[[89]](#cite_note-96) Norway shares a 1,619-kilometre (1,006 mi) land border with Sweden, 727 kilometres (452 mi) with Finland, and 196 kilometres (122 mi) with Russia to the east. To the north, west and south, Norway is bordered by the [Barents Sea](/wiki/Barents_Sea "Barents Sea"), the [Norwegian Sea](/wiki/Norwegian_Sea "Norwegian Sea"), the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"), and [Skagerrak](/wiki/Skagerrak "Skagerrak").[[90]](#cite_note-factbook-97) The [Scandinavian Mountains](/wiki/Scandinavian_Mountains "Scandinavian Mountains") form much of the border with Sweden.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Reine_Lofoten_2009.JPG/250px-Reine_Lofoten_2009.JPG)](/wiki/File:Reine_Lofoten_2009.JPG)

A fishing village on the island of [Moskenesøya](/wiki/Moskenes%C3%B8ya "Moskenesøya") in the [Lofoten](/wiki/Lofoten "Lofoten") archipelago

At 385,207 square kilometres (148,729 sq mi) (including [Svalbard](/wiki/Svalbard "Svalbard") and [Jan Mayen](/wiki/Jan_Mayen "Jan Mayen"); 323,808 square kilometres (125,023 sq mi) without),[[10]](#cite_note-kart_2019-14) much of the country is dominated by mountainous or high terrain, with a great variety of natural features caused by prehistoric [glaciers](/wiki/Glacier "Glacier") and varied [topography](/wiki/Topography "Topography"). The most noticeable of these are the fjords. [Sognefjorden](/wiki/Sognefjorden "Sognefjorden") is the world's second deepest fjord, and the world's longest at 204 kilometres (127 mi). The lake [Hornindalsvatnet](/wiki/Hornindalsvatnet "Hornindalsvatnet") is the deepest lake in Europe.[[91]](#cite_note-98) Norway has about 400,000 lakes[[92]](#cite_note-99)[[93]](#cite_note-100) and 239,057 registered islands.[[86]](#cite_note-SNL-93) [Permafrost](/wiki/Permafrost "Permafrost") can be found all year in the higher mountain areas and in the interior of Finnmark county. [Numerous glaciers](/wiki/List_of_glaciers_in_Norway "List of glaciers in Norway") are found in Norway. The land is mostly made of hard [granite](/wiki/Granite "Granite") and [gneiss](/wiki/Gneiss "Gneiss") rock, but [slate](/wiki/Slate "Slate"), [sandstone](/wiki/Sandstone "Sandstone"), and [limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone") are also common, and the lowest elevations contain marine deposits.

### Climate

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_NOR_1991%E2%80%932020.svg/250px-Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_NOR_1991%E2%80%932020.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Koppen-Geiger_Map_v2_NOR_1991%E2%80%932020.svg)

[Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") types of Norway 1991–2020 (0C/32F isotherm for coldest month dividing C and D climates).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Precipitation_normal_Norway.jpg/250px-Precipitation_normal_Norway.jpg)](/wiki/File:Precipitation_normal_Norway.jpg)

Map of Norway showing the normal precipitation (annual average). Period 1961–1990.

Because of the [Gulf Stream](/wiki/Gulf_Stream "Gulf Stream") and prevailing westerlies, Norway experiences higher temperatures and more precipitation than expected at such northern latitudes, especially along the coast. The mainland experiences four distinct seasons, with colder winters and less precipitation inland. The northernmost part has a mostly maritime [Subarctic climate](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate"), while Svalbard has an [Arctic](/wiki/Arctic "Arctic") [tundra](/wiki/Tundra "Tundra") climate. The southern and western parts of Norway, fully exposed to Atlantic storm fronts, experience more precipitation and have milder winters than the eastern and far northern parts. Areas to the east of the coastal mountains are in a [rain shadow](/wiki/Rain_shadow "Rain shadow"), and have lower rain and snow totals than the west. The lowlands around Oslo have the warmest summers, but also cold weather and snow in wintertime. The sunniest weather is along the south coast, but sometimes even the coast far north can be very sunny – the sunniest month with 430 sun hours was recorded in Tromsø.[[94]](#cite_note-Norwegian-Meteorological-Institute-101)[[95]](#cite_note-Book-Climate-102)

Because of Norway's high [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"), there are large seasonal variations in daylight. From late May to late July, the sun never completely descends beneath the horizon in areas north of the [Arctic Circle](/wiki/Arctic_Circle "Arctic Circle"), and the rest of the country experiences up to 20 hours of daylight per day. Conversely, from late November to late January, the sun never rises above the horizon in the north, and daylight hours are very short in the rest of the country.

Temperature anomalies found in coastal locations are exceptional, with southern Lofoten and [Bø Municipality](/wiki/B%C3%B8_Municipality_(Nordland) "Bø Municipality (Nordland)") having all monthly means above freezing in spite of being north of the Arctic Circle. The very northernmost coast of Norway would be ice-covered in winter if not for the Gulf Stream.[[96]](#cite_note-103) The east of the country has a more continental climate, and the mountain ranges have subarctic and tundra climates. There is also higher rainfall in areas exposed to the Atlantic, especially the western slopes of the mountain ranges and areas close, such as Bergen. The valleys east of the mountain ranges are the driest; some of the valleys are sheltered by mountains in most directions. [Saltdal Municipality](/wiki/Saltdal_Municipality "Saltdal Municipality") in Nordland is the driest place with 211 millimetres (8.3 inches) precipitation annually (1991–2020). In southern Norway, [Skjåk Municipality](/wiki/Skj%C3%A5k_Municipality "Skjåk Municipality") in Innlandet county gets 295 millimetres (11.6 inches) precipitation. [Finnmarksvidda](/wiki/Finnmarksvidda "Finnmarksvidda") and some interior valleys of [Troms](/wiki/Troms "Troms") county receive around 400 millimetres (16 inches) annually, and the high Arctic [Longyearbyen](/wiki/Longyearbyen "Longyearbyen") 217 millimetres (8.5 inches).[[97]](#cite_note-104)

Parts of southeastern Norway including parts of [Mjøsa](/wiki/Mj%C3%B8sa "Mjøsa") have a [humid continental climate](/wiki/Humid_continental_climate "Humid continental climate") ([Köppen](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification") Dfb), the southern and western coasts and also the coast north to Bodø have an [oceanic climate](/wiki/Oceanic_climate "Oceanic climate") (Cfb), and the outer coast further north almost to North Cape has a subpolar oceanic climate (Cfc). Further inland in the south and at higher altitudes, and also in much of Northern Norway, the [subarctic climate](/wiki/Subarctic_climate "Subarctic climate") (Dfc) dominates. A small strip of land along the coast east of North Cape (including Vardø) earlier had [tundra/alpine/polar climate](/wiki/Polar_climate "Polar climate") (ET), but this is mostly gone with the updated 1991–2020 climate normals, making this also subarctic. Large parts of Norway are covered by mountains and high altitude plateaus, and about one third of the land is above the treeline and thus exhibit [tundra/alpine/polar climate](/wiki/Tundra_climate "Tundra climate") (ET).[[94]](#cite_note-Norwegian-Meteorological-Institute-101)[[98]](#cite_note-105)[[99]](#cite_note-106)[[95]](#cite_note-Book-Climate-102)[[100]](#cite_note-107)

### Biodiversity

Main article: [Wildlife of Norway](/wiki/Wildlife_of_Norway "Wildlife of Norway")

Norway has a larger number of different [habitats](/wiki/Habitat "Habitat") than almost any other European country. There are approximately 60,000 species in Norway and adjacent waters (excluding bacteria and viruses). The Norwegian Shelf large marine ecosystem is considered highly productive.[[101]](#cite_note-108) The total number of species include 16,000 species of insects (probably 4,000 more species yet to be described), 20,000 species of [algae](/wiki/Algae "Algae"), 1,800 species of [lichen](/wiki/Lichen "Lichen"), 1,050 species of [mosses](/wiki/Moss "Moss"), 2,800 species of [vascular plants](/wiki/Vascular_plant "Vascular plant"), up to 7,000 species of [fungi](/wiki/Fungus "Fungus"), 450 species of birds (250 species nesting in Norway), 90 species of mammals, 45 fresh-water species of fish, 150 salt-water species of fish, 1,000 species of fresh-water [invertebrates](/wiki/Invertebrate "Invertebrate"), and 3,500 species of salt-water invertebrates.[[102]](#cite_note-109) About 40,000 of these species have been described by science. The [red list](/wiki/IUCN_Red_List "IUCN Red List") of 2010 encompasses 4,599 species.[[103]](#cite_note-red-110) Norway contains five terrestrial ecoregions: [Sarmatic mixed forests](/wiki/Sarmatic_mixed_forests "Sarmatic mixed forests"), [Scandinavian coastal conifer forests](/wiki/Scandinavian_coastal_conifer_forests "Scandinavian coastal conifer forests"), [Scandinavian and Russian taiga](/wiki/Scandinavian_and_Russian_taiga "Scandinavian and Russian taiga"), [Kola Peninsula tundra](/wiki/Kola_Peninsula_tundra "Kola Peninsula tundra"), and [Scandinavian montane birch forest and grasslands](/wiki/Scandinavian_montane_birch_forest_and_grasslands "Scandinavian montane birch forest and grasslands").[[104]](#cite_note-111)

Seventeen species are listed mainly because they are endangered on a global scale, such as the [European beaver](/wiki/Eurasian_beaver "Eurasian beaver"), even if the population in Norway is not seen as endangered. The number of threatened and near-threatened species equals to 3,682; it includes 418 fungi species, many of which are closely associated with the small remaining old-growth forests,[[105]](#cite_note-112) 36 bird species, and 16 species of mammals. In 2010, 2,398 species were listed as endangered or vulnerable; of these 1,250 were listed as vulnerable (VU), 871 as endangered (EN), and 276 species as critically endangered (CR), among which were the [grey wolf](/wiki/Grey_wolf "Grey wolf"), the [Arctic fox](/wiki/Arctic_fox "Arctic fox"), and the [pool frog](/wiki/Pool_frog "Pool frog").[[103]](#cite_note-red-110)

The largest predator in Norwegian waters is the [sperm whale](/wiki/Sperm_whale "Sperm whale"), and the largest fish is the [basking shark](/wiki/Basking_shark "Basking shark"). The largest predator on land is the [polar bear](/wiki/Polar_bear "Polar bear"), while the [brown bear](/wiki/Brown_bear "Brown bear") is the largest predator on the Norwegian mainland. The largest land animal on the mainland is the elk (American English: [moose](/wiki/Moose "Moose")).

### Environment

Attractive and dramatic scenery and landscape are found throughout Norway.[[106]](#cite_note-113) The west coast of southern Norway and the coast of northern Norway present some of the most visually impressive coastal sceneries in the world. [National Geographic](/wiki/National_Geographic_Society "National Geographic Society") has listed the Norwegian fjords as the world's top tourist attraction.[[107]](#cite_note-114) The country is also home to the natural phenomena of the [midnight sun](/wiki/Midnight_sun "Midnight sun") (during summer), as well as the [aurora borealis](/wiki/Aurora "Aurora") known also as the northern lights.[[108]](#cite_note-115)

The 2024 [Environmental Performance Index](/wiki/Environmental_Performance_Index "Environmental Performance Index") from [Yale University](/wiki/Yale_University "Yale University"), [Columbia University](/wiki/Columbia_University "Columbia University") and the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum") put Norway in seventh place, immediately below United Kingdom and Sweden.[[109]](#cite_note-116) The index is based on environmental risks to human health, habitat loss, and changes in CO2 emissions. The index notes over-exploitation of fisheries, but not [Norway's whaling](/wiki/Whaling_in_Norway "Whaling in Norway") or [oil exports](/wiki/Energy_in_Norway "Energy in Norway").[[110]](#cite_note-117) Norway had a 2019 [Forest Landscape Integrity Index](/wiki/Forest_Landscape_Integrity_Index "Forest Landscape Integrity Index") mean score of 6.98/10, ranking it 60th globally out of 172 countries.[[111]](#cite_note-118)

## Politics and government

Main articles: [Politics of Norway](/wiki/Politics_of_Norway "Politics of Norway") and [Law of Norway](/wiki/Law_of_Norway "Law of Norway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/65/REUNI%C3%93N_CON_EL_REY_DE_NORUEGA_HARALD_V_Y_HAAKON_MAGNUS%2C_PR%C3%8DNCIPE_HEREDERO_DE_NORUEGA._OSLO%2C_10_DE_DICIEMBRE_DE_2025._%2854975098432%29_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-REUNI%C3%93N_CON_EL_REY_DE_NORUEGA_HARALD_V_Y_HAAKON_MAGNUS%2C_PR%C3%8DNCIPE_HEREDERO_DE_NORUEGA._OSLO%2C_10_DE_DICIEMBRE_DE_2025._%2854975098432%29_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:REUNI%C3%93N_CON_EL_REY_DE_NORUEGA_HARALD_V_Y_HAAKON_MAGNUS,_PR%C3%8DNCIPE_HEREDERO_DE_NORUEGA._OSLO,_10_DE_DICIEMBRE_DE_2025._(54975098432)_(cropped).jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b4/Slottet_i_Oslo_1.jpg/250px-Slottet_i_Oslo_1.jpg)](/wiki/File:Slottet_i_Oslo_1.jpg)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bb/Storting_building.jpg/250px-Storting_building.jpg)](/wiki/File:Storting_building.jpg)

*[The Storting](/wiki/Storting_building "Storting building")* is the [Parliament of Norway](/wiki/Parliament_of_Norway "Parliament of Norway").

Norway is considered to be one of the most developed democracies and [states of justice](/wiki/Rechtsstaat "Rechtsstaat") in the world. According to [International IDEA](/wiki/International_Institute_for_Democracy_and_Electoral_Assistance "International Institute for Democracy and Electoral Assistance")’s Global State of Democracy (GSoD) Indices and Democracy Tracker, Norway performs in the high range on overall democratic measures, with particular strengths in elected government and gender equality.[[112]](#cite_note-119)[[113]](#cite_note-120)[[114]](#cite_note-121) Since 2010, Norway has been classified as the world's most democratic country by the [Democracy Index](/wiki/Democracy_Index "Democracy Index").[[115]](#cite_note-122)[[116]](#cite_note-123)[[117]](#cite_note-124)

According to the [Constitution of Norway](/wiki/Constitution_of_Norway "Constitution of Norway"), which was adopted on 17 May 1814[[118]](#cite_note-125) and was inspired by the [United States Declaration of Independence](/wiki/United_States_Declaration_of_Independence "United States Declaration of Independence") and [French Revolution](/wiki/French_Revolution "French Revolution"), Norway is a unitary [constitutional monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_monarchy "Constitutional monarchy") with a [parliamentary system](/wiki/Parliamentary_system "Parliamentary system") of government, wherein the [King of Norway](/wiki/Monarchy_of_Norway "Monarchy of Norway") is the [head of state](/wiki/Head_of_state "Head of state") and the [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Norway "Prime Minister of Norway") is the [head of government](/wiki/Head_of_government "Head of government"). Power is separated among the legislative, executive, and judicial branches of government, as defined by the Constitution, which serves as the country's supreme legal document.

The [monarch](/wiki/Norwegian_Royal_Family "Norwegian Royal Family") officially retains executive power. But following the introduction of a parliamentary system of government, the duties of the monarch became strictly representative and ceremonial.[[119]](#cite_note-126) The Monarch is [commander-in-chief](/wiki/Commander-in-chief "Commander-in-chief") of the [Norwegian Armed Forces](/wiki/Norwegian_Armed_Forces "Norwegian Armed Forces"), and serves as chief diplomatic official abroad and as a symbol of unity. [Harald V](/wiki/Harald_V_of_Norway "Harald V of Norway") of the [House of Schleswig-Holstein-Sonderburg-Glücksburg](/wiki/House_of_Gl%C3%BCcksburg "House of Glücksburg") ascended to the Norwegian throne in 1991, the first since the 14th century who has been born in the country.[[120]](#cite_note-norway.org-127) [Haakon, Crown Prince of Norway](/wiki/Haakon,_Crown_Prince_of_Norway "Haakon, Crown Prince of Norway"), is the heir to the throne.

In practice, the Prime Minister exercises the executive powers. Constitutionally, legislative power is vested with both the government and the Parliament of Norway, but the latter is the supreme legislature and a [unicameral](/wiki/Unicameralism "Unicameralism") body.[[121]](#cite_note-128) Norway is fundamentally structured as a [representative democracy](/wiki/Representative_democracy "Representative democracy"). The Parliament can pass a law by simple majority of the 169 representatives, of which 150 are elected directly from 19 constituencies, and an additional 19 seats ("levelling seats") are allocated on a nationwide basis to make the representation in parliament correspond better with the popular vote for the political parties. A 4% election threshold is required for a party to gain levelling seats in Parliament.[[122]](#cite_note-129)

The Parliament of Norway, called the *[Storting](/wiki/Parliament_of_Norway "Parliament of Norway")*, ratifies national [treaties](/wiki/Treaty "Treaty") developed by the executive branch. It can [impeach](/wiki/Impeachment "Impeachment") members of the government if their acts are declared unconstitutional. If an indicted suspect is impeached, Parliament has the power to remove the person from office.

The position of [prime minister](/wiki/Prime_Minister_of_Norway "Prime Minister of Norway") is allocated to the member of Parliament who can obtain the confidence of a majority in Parliament, usually the current leader of the largest political party or, more effectively, through a coalition of parties; Norway has often been ruled by minority governments. The prime minister nominates the cabinet, traditionally drawn from members of the same political party or parties in the Storting, making up the government. The PM organises the executive government and exercises its power as vested by the Constitution.[[123]](#cite_note-130)

Through the [Council of State](/wiki/Norwegian_Council_of_State "Norwegian Council of State"), a [privy council](/wiki/Privy_council "Privy council") presided over by the monarch, the prime minister and the cabinet meet at the [Royal Palace](/wiki/Royal_Palace,_Oslo "Royal Palace, Oslo") and formally consult the Monarch. All government bills need formal approval by the monarch before and after introduction to Parliament. The Council approves all of the monarch's actions as head of state.[[120]](#cite_note-norway.org-127)

Members of the Storting are directly elected from [party-list proportional representation](/wiki/Party-list_proportional_representation "Party-list proportional representation") in nineteen [multiwinner voting](/wiki/Multiwinner_voting "Multiwinner voting") constituencies in a national [multi-party system](/wiki/Multi-party_system "Multi-party system").[[124]](#cite_note-131) Historically, both the [Norwegian Labour Party](/wiki/Norwegian_Labour_Party "Norwegian Labour Party") and [Conservative Party](/wiki/Conservative_Party_(Norway) "Conservative Party (Norway)") have played leading political roles. In the early 21st century, the Labour Party has been in power since the [2005 election](/wiki/2005_Norwegian_parliamentary_election "2005 Norwegian parliamentary election"), in a [Red–Green Coalition](/wiki/Red%E2%80%93Green_Coalition "Red–Green Coalition") with the [Socialist Left Party](/wiki/Socialist_Left_Party_(Norway) "Socialist Left Party (Norway)") and the [Centre Party](/wiki/Centre_Party_(Norway) "Centre Party (Norway)").[[125]](#cite_note-132) Since 2005, both the Conservative Party and the [Progress Party](/wiki/Progress_Party_(Norway) "Progress Party (Norway)") have won numerous seats in the Parliament.[[126]](#cite_note-133)

In national elections in September 2013, two political parties, [Høyre](/wiki/H%C3%B8yre "Høyre") and [Fremskrittspartiet](/wiki/Progress_Party_(Norway) "Progress Party (Norway)"), were elected on promises of tax cuts, more spending on infrastructure and education, better services and stricter rules on immigration, formed a government. [Erna Solberg](/wiki/Erna_Solberg "Erna Solberg") became prime minister, the second female prime minister after [Gro Harlem Brundtland](/wiki/Gro_Harlem_Brundtland "Gro Harlem Brundtland") and the first conservative prime minister since [Jan P. Syse](/wiki/Jan_P._Syse "Jan P. Syse"). Solberg said her win was "a historic election victory for the right-wing parties".[[127]](#cite_note-134) Her centre-right government won re-election in the [2017 Norwegian parliamentary election](/wiki/2017_Norwegian_parliamentary_election "2017 Norwegian parliamentary election").[[79]](#cite_note-reuters.com-86) Norway's new centre-left cabinet under Prime Minister [Jonas Gahr Støre](/wiki/Jonas_Gahr_St%C3%B8re "Jonas Gahr Støre"), the leader of the Labour Party, took office on 14 October 2021.[[128]](#cite_note-135)

### Administrative divisions

Main articles: [Administrative divisions of Norway](/wiki/Administrative_divisions_of_Norway "Administrative divisions of Norway"), [Counties of Norway](/wiki/Counties_of_Norway "Counties of Norway"), [Municipalities of Norway](/wiki/Municipalities_of_Norway "Municipalities of Norway"), and [List of towns and cities in Norway](/wiki/List_of_towns_and_cities_in_Norway "List of towns and cities in Norway")

See also: [Sápmi (area)](/wiki/S%C3%A1pmi_(area) "Sápmi (area)")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a3/Fylkesinndeling2024_original.webp/250px-Fylkesinndeling2024_original.webp.png)](/wiki/File:Fylkesinndeling2024_original.webp)

A municipal and regional reform: "From 14 June 2022, the Storting decided the following division of counties."

Norway, a [unitary state](/wiki/Unitary_state "Unitary state"), is divided into fifteen first-level administrative [counties](/wiki/County "County") (*fylke*).[[129]](#cite_note-136) The counties are administered through directly elected [county councils](/wiki/County_municipality_(Norway) "County municipality (Norway)") who elect the [County Mayor](/wiki/County_mayor "County mayor"). Additionally, the [King](/wiki/Norwegian_Royal_Family "Norwegian Royal Family") and government are represented in every county by a [County Governor](/wiki/County_governor_(Norway) "County governor (Norway)") ([Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language"): *statsforvalteren*).[[130]](#cite_note-137) The counties are then sub-divided into 357 second-level municipalities ([Norwegian](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language"): *kommuner*), which in turn are administered by directly elected [municipal council](/wiki/Municipal_council_(Norway) "Municipal council (Norway)"), headed by a mayor and a small executive cabinet. The capital of [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo") is considered both a county and a municipality. Norway has two integral overseas territories out of mainland: [Jan Mayen](/wiki/Jan_Mayen "Jan Mayen") and [Svalbard](/wiki/Svalbard "Svalbard"), the only developed island in the archipelago of the same name, located far to the north of the Norwegian mainland.[[131]](#cite_note-138)

There are 108 settlements that have [town/city](/wiki/List_of_towns_and_cities_in_Norway "List of towns and cities in Norway") status in Norway (the Norwegian word *by* is used to represent these places and that word can be translated as either town or city in English). Cities/towns in Norway were historically designated by the King and used to have special rules and privileges under the law. This was changed in the late 20th century, so now towns/cities have no special rights and a municipality can designate an urban settlement as a city/town. Towns and cities in Norway do not have to be large. Some cities have over a million residents such as Oslo, while others are much smaller such as [Honningsvåg](/wiki/Honningsv%C3%A5g "Honningsvåg") with about 2,200 residents. Usually, there is only one town within a municipality, but there are some municipalities that have more than one town within it (such as [Larvik Municipality](/wiki/Larvik_Municipality "Larvik Municipality") which has the [town of Larvik](/wiki/Larvik_(town) "Larvik (town)") and the town of [Stavern](/wiki/Stavern "Stavern").[[132]](#cite_note-139)

#### Dependencies of Norway

Main article: [Dependencies of Norway](/wiki/Dependencies_of_Norway "Dependencies of Norway")

There are three [Antarctic](/wiki/Antarctica "Antarctica") and [Subantarctic](/wiki/Subantarctic "Subantarctic") [dependencies](/wiki/Dependent_territory "Dependent territory"): [Bouvet Island](/wiki/Bouvet_Island "Bouvet Island"), [Peter I Island](/wiki/Peter_I_Island "Peter I Island"), and [Queen Maud Land](/wiki/Queen_Maud_Land "Queen Maud Land"). On most maps, there was an unclaimed area between Queen Maud Land and the [South Pole](/wiki/South_Pole "South Pole") until 12 June 2015 when Norway formally annexed that area.[[133]](#cite_note-140)

### Judicial system and law enforcement

Main article: [Judiciary of Norway](/wiki/Judiciary_of_Norway "Judiciary of Norway")

Norway uses a [civil law system](/wiki/Civil_law_(legal_system) "Civil law (legal system)") where laws are created and amended in Parliament and the system regulated through the [Courts of justice of Norway](/wiki/Courts_of_justice_of_Norway "Courts of justice of Norway").
