It consists of the [Supreme Court](/wiki/Supreme_Court_of_Norway "Supreme Court of Norway") of 20 permanent judges and a [Chief Justice](/wiki/Chief_Justice_of_the_Supreme_Court_of_Norway "Chief Justice of the Supreme Court of Norway"), [appellate courts](/wiki/Appellate_court "Appellate court"), city and [district courts](/wiki/Courts_of_justice_of_Norway#District_courts "Courts of justice of Norway"), and [conciliation councils](/w/index.php?title=Conciliation_councils&action=edit&redlink=1 "Conciliation councils (page does not exist)").[[134]](#cite_note-norway-141) The judiciary is independent of executive and legislative branches. While the Prime Minister nominates Supreme Court Justices for office, their nomination must be approved by Parliament and formally confirmed by the Monarch. Usually, judges attached to regular courts are formally appointed by the Monarch on the advice of the Prime Minister.

The Courts' formal mission is to regulate the Norwegian judicial system, interpret the Constitution, and implement the legislation adopted by Parliament. In its judicial reviews, it monitors the legislative and executive branches to ensure that they comply with provisions of enacted legislation.[[134]](#cite_note-norway-141)

The [law is enforced in Norway](/wiki/Law_enforcement_in_Norway "Law enforcement in Norway") by the [Norwegian Police Service](/wiki/Norwegian_Police_Service "Norwegian Police Service"). It is a Unified National Police Service made up of 27 Police Districts and several specialist agencies, such as [Norwegian National Authority for the Investigation and Prosecution of Economic and Environmental Crime](/wiki/Norwegian_National_Authority_for_the_Investigation_and_Prosecution_of_Economic_and_Environmental_Crime "Norwegian National Authority for the Investigation and Prosecution of Economic and Environmental Crime"), known as *Økokrim*; and the [National Criminal Investigation Service](/wiki/National_Criminal_Investigation_Service_(Norway) "National Criminal Investigation Service (Norway)"), known as *Kripos*, each headed by a chief of police. The Police Service is headed by the [National Police Directorate](/wiki/National_Police_Directorate "National Police Directorate"), which reports to the Ministry of Justice and the Police. The Police Directorate is headed by a National Police Commissioner. The only exception is the [Norwegian Police Security Agency](/wiki/Norwegian_Police_Security_Service "Norwegian Police Security Service"), whose head answers directly to the Ministry of Justice and the Police.

Norway abolished the death penalty for regular criminal acts in 1902 and for high treason in war and war-crimes in 1979. Norwegian prisons are humane, rather than tough, with emphasis on rehabilitation. At 20%, Norway's re-conviction rate is among the lowest in the world.[[135]](#cite_note-142)

[Reporters Without Borders](/wiki/Reporters_Without_Borders "Reporters Without Borders"), in its 2024 [World Press Freedom Index](/wiki/World_Press_Freedom_Index "World Press Freedom Index"), ranked Norway in first place out of 180 countries.[[136]](#cite_note-143) In general, the legal and institutional framework in Norway is characterised by a high degree of transparency, accountability and integrity, and the perception and the occurrence of corruption are very low.[[137]](#cite_note-144)

### Human rights

Main article: [Human rights in Norway](/wiki/Human_rights_in_Norway "Human rights in Norway")

Norway has long been considered a progressive country, and has adopted legislation and policies to support women's rights, minority rights, and [LGBT rights](/wiki/LGBT_rights_in_Norway "LGBT rights in Norway"). As early as 1884, 171 of the leading figures, among them five Prime Ministers, co-founded the [Norwegian Association for Women's Rights](/wiki/Norwegian_Association_for_Women%27s_Rights "Norwegian Association for Women's Rights").[[138]](#cite_note-145) They successfully campaigned for women's [right to education](/wiki/Right_to_education "Right to education"), [women's suffrage](/wiki/Women%27s_suffrage "Women's suffrage"), the [right to work](/wiki/Right_to_work "Right to work"), and other gender equality policies. From the 1970s, gender equality also came high on the state agenda, with the establishment of a public body to promote gender equality, which evolved into the [Gender Equality and Anti-Discrimination Ombud](/wiki/Gender_Equality_and_Anti-Discrimination_Ombud "Gender Equality and Anti-Discrimination Ombud"). In the 21st century [intersectional feminists](/wiki/Intersectional_feminism "Intersectional feminism") have organised in the [Initiative for Inclusive Feminism](/wiki/Initiative_for_Inclusive_Feminism "Initiative for Inclusive Feminism"), which seeks to build a broad, intersectional feminist movement rooted in the universality and indivisibility of human rights.[[139]](#cite_note-146) It also maintains a very high quality of life ranking.[[140]](#cite_note-d493-147)

In 1990, the Norwegian constitution was amended to grant [absolute primogeniture](/wiki/Absolute_primogeniture "Absolute primogeniture") to the Norwegian throne, meaning that the eldest child, regardless of gender, takes precedence in the line of succession. As it was not retroactive, the current successor to the throne is the eldest son of the King, rather than his eldest child.[[141]](#cite_note-148)

The Sámi people have for centuries been the subject of discrimination and abuse by the dominant cultures in Scandinavia and Russia, those countries claiming possession of Sámi lands.[[142]](#cite_note-149) Norway has been greatly criticised by the international community for the politics of [Norwegianization](/wiki/Norwegianization "Norwegianization") of and discrimination against the indigenous population of the country.[[143]](#cite_note-150) Nevertheless, Norway was, in 1990, the first country to recognise [ILO-convention 1989](/wiki/Indigenous_and_Tribal_Peoples_Convention,_1989 "Indigenous and Tribal Peoples Convention, 1989") on [indigenous people](/wiki/Indigenous_people "Indigenous people") recommended by the UN.

Norway was the first country in the world to enact an anti-discrimination law protecting the rights of gay men and lesbians. In 1993, Norway became the second country to legalise [civil union](/wiki/Civil_union "Civil union") partnerships for same-sex couples, and on 1 January 2009, [Norway became the sixth country](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage_in_Norway "Same-sex marriage in Norway") to legalise [same-sex marriage](/wiki/Same-sex_marriage "Same-sex marriage").[[144]](#cite_note-151) As a promoter of human rights, Norway has held the annual [Oslo Freedom Forum](/wiki/Oslo_Freedom_Forum "Oslo Freedom Forum") conference, a gathering described by *[The Economist](/wiki/The_Economist "The Economist")* as "on its way to becoming a human-rights equivalent of the Davos economic forum".[[145]](#cite_note-152)

### Foreign relations

Main article: [Foreign relations of Norway](/wiki/Foreign_relations_of_Norway "Foreign relations of Norway")

See also: [Norway and the European Union](/wiki/Norway_and_the_European_Union "Norway and the European Union") and [Whaling in Norway](/wiki/Whaling_in_Norway "Whaling in Norway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/61/KNM_Fridtjof_Nansen-2006-06-01-side.jpg/250px-KNM_Fridtjof_Nansen-2006-06-01-side.jpg)](/wiki/File:KNM_Fridtjof_Nansen-2006-06-01-side.jpg)

[Royal Norwegian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Norwegian_Navy "Royal Norwegian Navy") [*Fridtjof Nansen* class frigate](/wiki/Fridtjof_Nansen-class_frigate "Fridtjof Nansen-class frigate")

Norway maintains embassies in 75 countries.[[146]](#cite_note-153) 73 countries maintain an embassy in Norway, all of them in the capital, Oslo.

Norway is a founding member of the [United Nations](/wiki/United_Nations "United Nations") (UN), the [North Atlantic Treaty Organization](/wiki/NATO "NATO") (NATO), the [Council of Europe](/wiki/Council_of_Europe "Council of Europe") and the [European Free Trade Association](/wiki/European_Free_Trade_Association "European Free Trade Association") (EFTA). Norway issued applications for accession to the European Union (EU) and its predecessors in 1962, 1967 and 1992, respectively. While Denmark, Sweden and Finland obtained membership, the Norwegian electorate rejected the treaties of accession in referendums in [1972](/wiki/1972_Norwegian_European_Communities_membership_referendum "1972 Norwegian European Communities membership referendum") and [1994](/wiki/1994_Norwegian_European_Union_membership_referendum "1994 Norwegian European Union membership referendum").

After the 1994 referendum, Norway maintained its membership in the [European Economic Area](/wiki/European_Economic_Area "European Economic Area") (EEA), granting the country access to the [internal market](/wiki/Single_market_of_the_European_Union "Single market of the European Union") of the Union, on the condition that Norway implements the Union's pieces of legislation which are deemed relevant.[[147]](#cite_note-154) Successive Norwegian governments have, since 1994, requested participation in parts of the EU's co-operation that go beyond the provisions of the EEA agreement. Non-voting participation by Norway has been granted in, for instance, the Union's [Common Security and Defence Policy](/wiki/Common_Security_and_Defence_Policy "Common Security and Defence Policy"), the [Schengen Agreement](/wiki/Schengen_Agreement "Schengen Agreement"), and the [European Defence Agency](/wiki/European_Defence_Agency "European Defence Agency"), as well as 19 separate programmes.[[148]](#cite_note-155)

Norway participated in the 1990s brokering of the [Oslo Accords](/wiki/Oslo_Accords "Oslo Accords"), an unsuccessful attempt to resolve the [Israeli–Palestinian conflict](/wiki/Israeli%E2%80%93Palestinian_conflict "Israeli–Palestinian conflict").

### Military

Main article: [Norwegian Armed Forces](/wiki/Norwegian_Armed_Forces "Norwegian Armed Forces")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/67/First_Norwegian_F-35.png/250px-First_Norwegian_F-35.png)](/wiki/File:First_Norwegian_F-35.png)

The first Norwegian [F-35 Lightning II](/wiki/F-35_Lightning_II "F-35 Lightning II") lands at Luke Air Force Base.

The Norwegian Armed Forces numbers about 25,000 personnel, including civilian employees. According to 2009 mobilisation plans, full mobilisation produces approximately 83,000 combatant personnel. Norway has [conscription](/wiki/Conscription "Conscription") (including 6–12 months of training);[[149]](#cite_note-156) in 2013, the country became the first in Europe and NATO to draft women as well as men. However, due to less need for conscripts after the [Cold War](/wiki/Cold_War "Cold War"), few people have to serve if they are not motivated.[[150]](#cite_note-157) The Armed Forces are subordinate to the [Norwegian Ministry of Defence](/wiki/Norwegian_Ministry_of_Defence "Norwegian Ministry of Defence"). The Commander-in-Chief is [King Harald V](/wiki/Harald_V_of_Norway "Harald V of Norway"). The military of Norway is divided into the [Norwegian Army](/wiki/Norwegian_Army "Norwegian Army"), the [Royal Norwegian Navy](/wiki/Royal_Norwegian_Navy "Royal Norwegian Navy"), the [Royal Norwegian Air Force](/wiki/Royal_Norwegian_Air_Force "Royal Norwegian Air Force"), the [Norwegian Cyber Defence Force](/wiki/Norwegian_Cyber_Defence_Force "Norwegian Cyber Defence Force") and the [Home Guard](/wiki/Home_Guard_(Norway) "Home Guard (Norway)").

The country was one of the founding nations of the [North Atlantic Treaty Organization](/wiki/NATO "NATO") (NATO) on 4 April 1949. Norway contributed in the [International Security Assistance Force](/wiki/International_Security_Assistance_Force "International Security Assistance Force") (ISAF) in [Afghanistan](/wiki/War_in_Afghanistan_(2001%E2%80%93present) "War in Afghanistan (2001–present)").[[151]](#cite_note-158) Additionally, Norway has contributed in several missions in contexts of the United Nations, NATO, and the [Common Security and Defence Policy](/wiki/Common_Security_and_Defence_Policy "Common Security and Defence Policy") of the European Union.

## Economy

Main articles: [Economy of Norway](/wiki/Economy_of_Norway "Economy of Norway"), [Energy in Norway](/wiki/Energy_in_Norway "Energy in Norway"), [European Economic Area](/wiki/European_Economic_Area "European Economic Area"), and [Exclusive economic zone § Norway](/wiki/Exclusive_economic_zone#Norway "Exclusive economic zone")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/b3/Territorial_waters_-_Norway.svg/250px-Territorial_waters_-_Norway.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Territorial_waters_-_Norway.svg)

Norway's claimed economic zones

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/ad/EU_and_EFTA.svg/250px-EU_and_EFTA.svg.png)](/wiki/File:EU_and_EFTA.svg)

Members of the [European Free Trade Association](/wiki/European_Free_Trade_Association "European Free Trade Association") (green) participate in the [European Single Market](/wiki/European_Single_Market "European Single Market") and are part of the [Schengen Area](/wiki/Schengen_Area "Schengen Area").

Norwegians enjoy the second-highest [GDP per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(nominal)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (nominal) per capita") among European countries (after [Luxembourg](/wiki/Luxembourg "Luxembourg")), and the sixth-highest [PPP-adjusted GDP per capita](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_GDP_(PPP)_per_capita "List of countries by GDP (PPP) per capita") in the world. Norway ranks as the second-wealthiest country in monetary value, with the largest capital reserve per capita of any nation.[[152]](#cite_note-159) According to the CIA World Factbook, Norway is a net external creditor of debt.[[90]](#cite_note-factbook-97) Norway reclaimed first place in the world in the [UNDP](/wiki/United_Nations_Development_Programme "United Nations Development Programme") [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") (HDI) in 2009.[[153]](#cite_note-160) The standard of living in Norway is among the highest in the world. *[Foreign Policy](/wiki/Foreign_Policy "Foreign Policy")* magazine ranks Norway last in its [Failed States Index](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_Failed_States_Index "List of countries by Failed States Index") for 2009 and 2023, judging Norway to be the world's most well-functioning and stable country. The [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD") ranks Norway fourth in the 2013 equalised [Better Life Index](/wiki/OECD_Better_Life_Index "OECD Better Life Index") and third in intergenerational earnings elasticity according to a 2010 study.[[154]](#cite_note-161)[[155]](#cite_note-162)

The Norwegian economy is an example of a [mixed economy](/wiki/Mixed_economy "Mixed economy"); a prosperous capitalist [welfare state](/wiki/Welfare_state "Welfare state"), it features a combination of [free market](/wiki/Free_market "Free market") activity and large state ownership in certain key sectors, influenced by both liberal governments from the late 19th century and later by [social democratic](/wiki/Labour_Party_(Norway) "Labour Party (Norway)") governments in the postwar era.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] [Public healthcare in Norway](/wiki/Healthcare_in_Norway "Healthcare in Norway") is free (after an annual charge of around 2000 [kroner](/wiki/Norwegian_krone "Norwegian krone") for those over 16), and parents have 46 weeks paid[[156]](#cite_note-163) parental leave. The state income derived from natural resources includes a significant contribution from petroleum production. As of February 2025[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Norway&action=edit), Norway has an unemployment rate of 3.9%, with 69.7% of the population aged 15–74 employed.[[157]](#cite_note-164) People in the labour force are either employed or looking for work.[[158]](#cite_note-165) As of 2023[[update]](https://en.wikipedia.org/w/index.php?title=Norway&action=edit), 10.6% of the population aged 18–67 receive a disability benefit[[159]](#cite_note-166) and 30% of the labour force are employed by the government, the highest in the [OECD](/wiki/OECD "OECD").[[160]](#cite_note-167) The hourly productivity levels, as well as average hourly wages in Norway, are among the highest in the world.[[161]](#cite_note-168)[[162]](#cite_note-169)

The [egalitarian](/wiki/Egalitarianism "Egalitarianism") values of Norwegian society have kept the wage difference between the lowest paid worker and the CEO of most companies as much less than in comparable western economies.[[163]](#cite_note-170) This is also evident in [Norway's low Gini coefficient](/wiki/List_of_countries_by_income_equality "List of countries by income equality").

The state has large ownership positions in key industrial sectors, such as the strategic petroleum sector (Equinor), hydroelectric energy production ([Statkraft](/wiki/Statkraft "Statkraft")), aluminium production ([Norsk Hydro](/wiki/Norsk_Hydro "Norsk Hydro")), the largest Norwegian bank ([DNB](/wiki/DNB_ASA "DNB ASA")), and telecommunication provider ([Telenor](/wiki/Telenor "Telenor")). Through these big companies, the government controls approximately 30% of the stock values at the Oslo Stock Exchange.[[164]](#cite_note-171) When non-listed companies are included, the state has even higher share in ownership (mainly from direct oil licence ownership).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Norway is a major shipping nation and has the world's sixth largest [merchant fleet](/wiki/Ship_transport "Ship transport"), with 1,412 Norwegian-owned merchant vessels.[[165]](#cite_note-172)

By referendums in [1972](/wiki/1972_Norwegian_European_Communities_membership_referendum "1972 Norwegian European Communities membership referendum") and [1994](/wiki/1994_Norwegian_European_Union_membership_referendum "1994 Norwegian European Union membership referendum"), Norwegians rejected proposals to join the European Union (EU). However, Norway, together with [Iceland](/wiki/Iceland "Iceland") and [Liechtenstein](/wiki/Liechtenstein "Liechtenstein"), participates in the European Union's single market through the [European Economic Area](/wiki/European_Economic_Area "European Economic Area") (EEA) agreement. The EEA Treaty between the European Union countries and the EFTA countries—transposed into Norwegian law via "EØS-loven"[[166]](#cite_note-173)—describes the procedures for implementing European Union rules in Norway and the other EFTA countries. Norway is a highly integrated member of most sectors of the EU internal market. Some sectors, such as agriculture, oil and fish, are not wholly covered by the EEA Treaty. Norway has also acceded to the [Schengen Agreement](/wiki/Schengen_Agreement "Schengen Agreement") and several other intergovernmental agreements among the EU member states.

The country is richly endowed with natural resources including petroleum, [hydropower](/wiki/Hydropower "Hydropower"), fish, [forests](/wiki/Forestry "Forestry"), and minerals. Large reserves of petroleum and natural gas were discovered in the 1960s, which led to an economic boom.[[167]](#cite_note-174) Norway has obtained one of the highest standards of living in the world in part by having a large amount of natural resources compared to the size of the population.[[168]](#cite_note-175) In 2011, 28% of state revenues were generated from the petroleum industry.[[169]](#cite_note-176)[*[failed verification](/wiki/Wikipedia:Verifiability "Wikipedia:Verifiability")*]

Norway was the first country to ban deforestation, with a view to preventing its rain forests from vanishing. The country declared its intention at the UN Climate Summit in 2014 alongside Great Britain and Germany.[[170]](#cite_note-177)

### Resources

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/6/6b/Gaulosen_og_%C3%98ysand.jpg/250px-Gaulosen_og_%C3%98ysand.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gaulosen_og_%C3%98ysand.jpg)

Agriculture is a significant sector, in spite of the mountainous landscape ([Øysand](/wiki/%C3%98ysand "Øysand")).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/36/Stockfish_in_Lofoten.jpg/250px-Stockfish_in_Lofoten.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stockfish_in_Lofoten.jpg)

[Stockfish](/wiki/Stockfish "Stockfish") has been exported from [Lofoten](/wiki/Lofoten "Lofoten") in Norway for at least 1,000 years.

#### Oil industry

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Heidrun%2C_Norskehavet_%2828276260560%29.jpg/250px-Heidrun%2C_Norskehavet_%2828276260560%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Heidrun,_Norskehavet_(28276260560).jpg)

Oil production has been central to the Norwegian economy since the 1970s, with a dominating [state ownership](/wiki/State_ownership "State ownership") ([Heidrun oil field](/wiki/Heidrun_oil_field "Heidrun oil field")).

Export revenues from oil and gas have risen to over 40% of total exports and constitute almost 20% of the GDP.[[171]](#cite_note-178) Norway is the fifth-largest oil exporter and third-largest gas exporter in the world, but it is not a member of [OPEC](/wiki/OPEC "OPEC"). In 1995, the Norwegian government established the sovereign wealth fund (["Government Pension Fund – Global"](/wiki/The_Government_Pension_Fund_of_Norway "The Government Pension Fund of Norway")) to be funded with oil revenues.

The government controls its petroleum resources through a combination of state ownership in major operators in the oil fields (with approximately 62% ownership in Equinor in 2007) and the fully state-owned [Petoro](/wiki/Petoro "Petoro"), which has a market value of about twice Equinor, and [SDFI](/wiki/State%27s_Direct_Financial_Interest "State's Direct Financial Interest"). Finally, the government controls licensing of exploration and production of fields. The fund invests in developed financial markets outside Norway. Spending from the fund is constrained by the budgetary rule (*Handlingsregelen*), which limits spending over time to no more than the real value yield of the fund, lowered in 2017 to 3% of the fund's total value.[[172]](#cite_note-179)

Between 1966 and 2013, Norwegian companies drilled 5,085 oil wells, mostly in the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea").[[173]](#cite_note-180) Oil fields not yet in the production phase include: [Wisting Central](/w/index.php?title=Wisting_Central&action=edit&redlink=1 "Wisting Central (page does not exist)")—calculated size in 2013 at 65–156 million barrels of oil and 10 to 40 billion cubic feet (0.28 to 1.13 billion cubic metres), (*utvinnbar*) of gas.[[174]](#cite_note-WistingCentral-181) and the [Castberg Oil Field](/w/index.php?title=Castberg_Oil_Field&action=edit&redlink=1 "Castberg Oil Field (page does not exist)") (*Castberg-feltet*[[174]](#cite_note-WistingCentral-181))—calculated size at 540 million barrels of oil, and 2 to 7 billion cubic feet (57 to 198 million cubic metres) (*utvinnbar*) of gas.[[175]](#cite_note-182) Both oil fields are located in the [Barents Sea](/wiki/Barents_Sea "Barents Sea").

Norway is also the world's second-largest exporter of fish (in value, after China).[[176]](#cite_note-183)[[177]](#cite_note-184) Fish from fish farms and catch constitutes the second largest (behind oil/natural gas) export product measured in value.[[178]](#cite_note-185)[[179]](#cite_note-186) Norway is the world's largest producer of salmon, followed by [Chile](/wiki/Chile "Chile").[[180]](#cite_note-187)

[Hydroelectric plants](/wiki/Hydroelectricity "Hydroelectricity") generate roughly 98–99% of Norway's electric power, more than any other country in the world.[[181]](#cite_note-188)

Norway contains significant mineral resources, and in 2013, its mineral production was valued at US$1.5 billion (Norwegian Geological Survey data). The most valuable minerals are calcium carbonate ([limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone")), building stone, [nepheline syenite](/wiki/Nepheline_syenite "Nepheline syenite"), [olivine](/wiki/Olivine "Olivine"), iron, [titanium](/wiki/Titanium "Titanium"), and [nickel](/wiki/Nickel "Nickel").[[182]](#cite_note-189)

In 2017, the Government Pension Fund controlled assets surpassed a value of US$1 trillion (equal to US$190,000 per capita),[[183]](#cite_note-190) about 250% of Norway's 2017 GDP.[[184]](#cite_note-191) It is the largest [sovereign wealth fund](/wiki/Sovereign_wealth_fund "Sovereign wealth fund") in the world.[[185]](#cite_note-192)

Other nations with economies based on natural resources, such as Russia, are trying to learn from Norway by establishing similar funds. The investment choices of the Norwegian fund are directed by [ethical guidelines](/wiki/Socially_responsible_investing "Socially responsible investing"); for example, the fund is not allowed to invest in companies that produce parts for nuclear weapons. Norway's highly [transparent](/wiki/Transparency_(market) "Transparency (market)") investment scheme[[186]](#cite_note-193) is lauded by the international community.[[187]](#cite_note-194)

### Transport

Main articles: [Transport in Norway](/wiki/Transport_in_Norway "Transport in Norway"), [Rail transport in Norway](/wiki/Rail_transport_in_Norway "Rail transport in Norway"), and [List of airports in Norway](/wiki/List_of_airports_in_Norway "List of airports in Norway")

Due to the low population density, narrow shape and long coastlines of Norway, its public transport is less developed than in many European countries, especially outside the major cities. The country has long-standing water transport traditions, but the [Norwegian Ministry of Transport and Communications](/wiki/Norwegian_Ministry_of_Transport_and_Communications "Norwegian Ministry of Transport and Communications") has in recent years implemented rail, road, and air transport through numerous subsidiaries to develop the country's infrastructure.[[188]](#cite_note-195) Under discussion is development of a new high-speed rail system between the nation's largest cities.[[189]](#cite_note-196)[[190]](#cite_note-197)

Norway's main railway network consists of 4,114 kilometres (2,556 mi) of [standard gauge](/wiki/Standard_gauge "Standard gauge") lines, of which 242 kilometres (150 mi) is [double track](/wiki/Double_track "Double track") and 64 kilometres (40 mi) [high-speed rail](/wiki/High-speed_rail "High-speed rail") (210 km/h) while 62% is electrified at [15 kV  16.7 Hz AC](/wiki/15_kV_AC_railway_electrification "15 kV AC railway electrification"). In 2023, the railways transported 78,220,000 passengers, 3,153 million [passenger-kilometres](/wiki/Units_of_transportation_measurement "Units of transportation measurement"), and 32,230,000 tonnes of cargo for 3,928 million tonne-kilometres.[[191]](#cite_note-198) The entire network is owned by [Bane NOR](/wiki/Bane_NOR "Bane NOR").[[192]](#cite_note-199) Domestic passenger trains are operated by various companies, including [Vy](/wiki/Vy "Vy"), [SJ](/wiki/SJ_AB#SJ_Norge "SJ AB"), [Go-Ahead](/wiki/Go-Ahead_Nordic "Go-Ahead Nordic") and [Flytoget](/wiki/Flytoget "Flytoget"), while freight trains are operated by [CargoNet](/wiki/CargoNet "CargoNet") and [OnRail](/w/index.php?title=OnRail&action=edit&redlink=1 "OnRail (page does not exist)").[[193]](#cite_note-200)

Investment in new infrastructure and maintenance is financed through the state budget,[[194]](#cite_note-201) and subsidies are provided for passenger train operations.[[195]](#cite_note-202) NSB operates long-haul trains, including [night trains](/wiki/NSB_Night_Train "NSB Night Train"), regional services and four [commuter train](/wiki/Commuter_rail "Commuter rail") systems, around [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo_Commuter_Rail "Oslo Commuter Rail"), [Trondheim](/wiki/Tr%C3%B8ndelag_Commuter_Rail "Trøndelag Commuter Rail"), [Bergen](/w/index.php?title=Bergen_Commuter_Rail&action=edit&redlink=1 "Bergen Commuter Rail (page does not exist)") and [Stavanger](/wiki/J%C3%A6ren_Line "Jæren Line").[[196]](#cite_note-203)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/Oslo_Airport_terminal_night_view.jpg/250px-Oslo_Airport_terminal_night_view.jpg)](/wiki/File:Oslo_Airport_terminal_night_view.jpg)

[Oslo Airport, Gardermoen](/wiki/Oslo_Airport,_Gardermoen "Oslo Airport, Gardermoen")

Norway has approximately 95,120 kilometres (59,100 mi) of road network, of which 72,033 kilometres (44,759 mi) are paved and 664 kilometres (413 mi) are motorway.[[90]](#cite_note-factbook-97) The four tiers of road routes are national, county, municipal and private, with national and primary county roads numbered en route. The most important national routes are part of the [European route](/wiki/International_E-road_network "International E-road network") scheme. The two most prominent are the [European route E6](/wiki/European_route_E6 "European route E6") going north–south through the entire country, and the [E39](/wiki/European_route_E39 "European route E39"), which follows the West Coast. National and county roads are managed by the [Norwegian Public Roads Administration](/wiki/Norwegian_Public_Roads_Administration "Norwegian Public Roads Administration").[[197]](#cite_note-204)

Norway has the world's largest registered stock of [plug-in electric vehicles per capita](/wiki/Plug-in_electric_vehicles_in_Norway "Plug-in electric vehicles in Norway").[[198]](#cite_note-205)[[199]](#cite_note-206)[[200]](#cite_note-207) In March 2014, Norway became the first country where over 1 in every 100 passenger cars on the roads is a plug-in electric.[[201]](#cite_note-208) The plug-in electric segment [market share](/wiki/Market_share "Market share") of new car sales is also the highest in the world.[[202]](#cite_note-209) According to a report by *[Dagens Næringsliv](/wiki/Dagens_N%C3%A6ringsliv "Dagens Næringsliv")* in June 2016, the country would like to ban sales of gasoline and diesel powered vehicles as early as 2025.[[203]](#cite_note-210)

Of the 146 airports in Norway,[[90]](#cite_note-factbook-97) 52 are public,[[204]](#cite_note-avinorpassengers-211) and 43 are operated by the state-owned [Avinor](/wiki/Avinor "Avinor").[[205]](#cite_note-212) [Seven airports](/wiki/List_of_the_largest_airports_in_the_Nordic_countries "List of the largest airports in the Nordic countries") have more than one million passengers annually.[[204]](#cite_note-avinorpassengers-211) A total of 41,089,675 passengers passed through Norwegian airports in 2007, of whom 13,397,458 were international.[[204]](#cite_note-avinorpassengers-211)

The central gateway to Norway by air is [Oslo Airport, Gardermoen](/wiki/Oslo_Airport,_Gardermoen "Oslo Airport, Gardermoen").[[204]](#cite_note-avinorpassengers-211) Located about 35 kilometres (22 mi) northeast of Oslo, it is [hub](/wiki/Airline_hub "Airline hub") for the two major Norwegian airlines: [Scandinavian Airlines](/wiki/Scandinavian_Airlines "Scandinavian Airlines")[[206]](#cite_note-213) and [Norwegian Air Shuttle](/wiki/Norwegian_Air_Shuttle "Norwegian Air Shuttle"),[[207]](#cite_note-214) and for regional aircraft from Western Norway.[[208]](#cite_note-215) There are departures to most European countries and some intercontinental destinations.[[209]](#cite_note-216)[[210]](#cite_note-217) A direct high-speed train connects to Oslo Central Station every 10 minutes for a 20 min ride.

### Research

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/80/Niels_Henrik_Abel.jpg/250px-Niels_Henrik_Abel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Niels_Henrik_Abel.jpg)

[Niels Henrik Abel](/wiki/Niels_Henrik_Abel "Niels Henrik Abel") made pioneering contributions in a variety of fields. The [Abel Prize](/wiki/Abel_Prize "Abel Prize") in mathematics, originally proposed in 1899 to complement the [Nobel Prizes](/wiki/Nobel_Prize "Nobel Prize"), is named in his honour.

Norway has a rich history of contributions to science, mathematics, and technology, with several internationally recognised scientists and innovators.

In mathematics, [Niels Henrik Abel](/wiki/Niels_Henrik_Abel "Niels Henrik Abel") and [Sophus Lie](/wiki/Sophus_Lie "Sophus Lie") made groundbreaking contributions to analysis and [group theory](/wiki/Group_theory "Group theory"). [Caspar Wessel](/wiki/Caspar_Wessel "Caspar Wessel") was the first to describe [vectors](/wiki/Vector_space "Vector space") and [complex numbers](/wiki/Complex_number "Complex number") in the [complex plane](/wiki/Complex_plane "Complex plane"), laying the foundation for modern vector and complex analysis. [Thoralf Skolem](/wiki/Thoralf_Skolem "Thoralf Skolem") made revolutionary contributions to [mathematical logic](/wiki/Mathematical_logic "Mathematical logic"), while [Øystein Ore](/wiki/%C3%98ystein_Ore "Øystein Ore") and [Ludwig Sylow](/wiki/Peter_Ludvig_Sylow "Peter Ludvig Sylow") advanced group theory. [Atle Selberg](/wiki/Atle_Selberg "Atle Selberg"), a major figure in 20th-century mathematics, was honoured with the [Fields Medal](/wiki/Fields_Medal "Fields Medal"), [Wolf Prize](/wiki/Wolf_Prize_in_Mathematics "Wolf Prize in Mathematics"), and [Abel Prize](/wiki/Abel_Prize "Abel Prize"). [Ernst S. Selmer](/wiki/Ernst_Sejersted_Selmer "Ernst Sejersted Selmer")'s work significantly influenced modern [cryptographic algorithms](/wiki/Cryptographic_algorithm "Cryptographic algorithm").

In physics, notable figures include [Kristian Birkeland](/wiki/Kristian_Birkeland "Kristian Birkeland"), known for his work on the [aurora borealis](/wiki/Aurora "Aurora"), and [Ivar Giaever](/wiki/Ivar_Giaever "Ivar Giaever"), a Nobel laureate in physics. [Carl Anton Bjerknes](/wiki/Carl_Anton_Bjerknes "Carl Anton Bjerknes") and [Christopher Hansteen](/wiki/Christopher_Hansteen "Christopher Hansteen") made contributions to [hydrodynamics](/wiki/Hydrodynamics "Hydrodynamics") and [geomagnetism](/wiki/Earth%27s_magnetic_field "Earth's magnetic field"), respectively. The meteorologists [Vilhelm Bjerknes](/wiki/Vilhelm_Bjerknes "Vilhelm Bjerknes") and [Ragnar Fjørtoft](/wiki/Ragnar_Fj%C3%B8rtoft "Ragnar Fjørtoft") were instrumental in the development of [numerical weather prediction](/wiki/Numerical_weather_prediction "Numerical weather prediction").

Norwegian chemists like [Lars Onsager](/wiki/Lars_Onsager "Lars Onsager"), a Nobel laureate, and [Odd Hassel](/wiki/Odd_Hassel "Odd Hassel"), recognised for his work in [stereochemistry](/wiki/Stereochemistry "Stereochemistry"), have left a lasting legacy. [Peter Waage](/wiki/Peter_Waage "Peter Waage") and [Cato Maximilian Guldberg](/wiki/Cato_Maximilian_Guldberg "Cato Maximilian Guldberg") formulated the [law of mass action](/wiki/Law_of_mass_action "Law of mass action"), fundamental to chemical reaction theory.

In technology, [Victor Goldschmidt](/wiki/Victor_Goldschmidt "Victor Goldschmidt") is regarded as a founder of modern [geochemistry](/wiki/Geochemistry "Geochemistry"). [Håkon Wium Lie](/wiki/H%C3%A5kon_Wium_Lie "Håkon Wium Lie") pioneered [Cascading Style Sheets](/wiki/CSS "CSS") (CSS), a cornerstone of web design. [Pål Spilling](/wiki/P%C3%A5l_Spilling "Pål Spilling") contributed to the development of the [Internet Protocol](/wiki/Internet_Protocol "Internet Protocol"), bringing the Internet to Europe. Computer scientists [Ole-Johan Dahl](/wiki/Ole-Johan_Dahl "Ole-Johan Dahl") and [Kristen Nygaard](/wiki/Kristen_Nygaard "Kristen Nygaard") developed [Simula](/wiki/Simula "Simula"), the first [object-oriented programming language](/wiki/Object-oriented_programming "Object-oriented programming"), earning them the prestigious [Turing Award](/wiki/Turing_Award "Turing Award").

Norwegian academics have also advanced social sciences. [Arne Næss](/wiki/Arne_N%C3%A6ss "Arne Næss") founded [deep ecology](/wiki/Deep_ecology "Deep ecology"), while [Johan Galtung](/wiki/Johan_Galtung "Johan Galtung") established the field of [peace studies](/wiki/Peace_and_conflict_studies "Peace and conflict studies"). Criminologists [Nils Christie](/wiki/Nils_Christie "Nils Christie") and [Thomas Mathiesen](/wiki/Thomas_Mathiesen "Thomas Mathiesen"), sociologists [Vilhelm Aubert](/wiki/Vilhelm_Aubert "Vilhelm Aubert"), [Harriet Holter](/wiki/Harriet_Holter "Harriet Holter"), and [Erik Grønseth](/wiki/Erik_Gr%C3%B8nseth "Erik Grønseth"), and political scientist [Stein Rokkan](/wiki/Stein_Rokkan "Stein Rokkan") made pioneering contributions to their fields. Economists [Ragnar Frisch](/wiki/Ragnar_Frisch "Ragnar Frisch"), [Trygve Haavelmo](/wiki/Trygve_Haavelmo "Trygve Haavelmo"), and [Finn E. Kydland](/wiki/Finn_E._Kydland "Finn E. Kydland") were honoured with Nobel Prizes for their work in [econometrics](/wiki/Econometrics "Econometrics") and [macroeconomics](/wiki/Macroeconomics "Macroeconomics").

As of 2025, Norway is ranked 20th in the [Global Innovation Index](/wiki/Global_Innovation_Index "Global Innovation Index") in 2025.[[211]](#cite_note-218)[[212]](#cite_note-219) The country has produced fourteen Nobel laureates across various disciplines.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Tourism

Main article: [Tourism in Norway](/wiki/Tourism_in_Norway "Tourism in Norway")

See also: [Tourist attractions in Norway](/wiki/Category:Tourist_attractions_in_Norway "Category:Tourist attractions in Norway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/da/Geirangerfjorden_from_%C3%98rnesvingen%2C_Stranda%2C_M%C3%B8re_og_Romsdal%2C_Norway%2C_2025_May.jpg/250px-Geirangerfjorden_from_%C3%98rnesvingen%2C_Stranda%2C_M%C3%B8re_og_Romsdal%2C_Norway%2C_2025_May.jpg)](/wiki/File:Geirangerfjorden_from_%C3%98rnesvingen,_Stranda,_M%C3%B8re_og_Romsdal,_Norway,_2025_May.jpg)

The [Geirangerfjord](/wiki/Geirangerfjord "Geirangerfjord") in [Møre og Romsdal](/wiki/M%C3%B8re_og_Romsdal "Møre og Romsdal"), since 2005 on [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")'s list of [World Heritage Sites](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site").

In 2019, Norway ranked 20th in the [World Economic Forum](/wiki/World_Economic_Forum "World Economic Forum")'s [Travel and Tourism Competitiveness Report](/wiki/Travel_and_Tourism_Competitiveness_Report "Travel and Tourism Competitiveness Report").[[213]](#cite_note-220) Tourism in Norway contributed to 4.2% of the gross domestic product as reported in 2016.[[214]](#cite_note-Statistics_Norway-2016-221) Every one in fifteen people throughout the country work in the tourism industry.[[214]](#cite_note-Statistics_Norway-2016-221) Tourism is seasonal in Norway, with more than half of total tourists visiting between the months of May and August.[[214]](#cite_note-Statistics_Norway-2016-221)

The main attractions of Norway are the varied landscapes that extend across the [Arctic Circle](/wiki/Arctic_Circle "Arctic Circle"). It is famous for its coastline and its mountains, ski resorts, lakes and woods. Popular tourist destinations in Norway include [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo"), [Ålesund](/wiki/%C3%85lesund_(town) "Ålesund (town)"), [Bergen](/wiki/Bergen_(city) "Bergen (city)"), [Stavanger](/wiki/Stavanger_(city) "Stavanger (city)"), [Trondheim](/wiki/Trondheim_(city) "Trondheim (city)"), [Kristiansand](/wiki/Kristiansand_(town) "Kristiansand (town)"), [Arendal](/wiki/Arendal_(town) "Arendal (town)"), [Tromsø](/wiki/Troms%C3%B8_(city) "Tromsø (city)"), [Fredrikstad](/wiki/Fredrikstad_(town) "Fredrikstad (town)"), and [Tønsberg](/wiki/T%C3%B8nsberg "Tønsberg"). Much of the nature of Norway remains unspoiled, and thus attracts numerous hikers and skiers. The fjords, mountains and waterfalls in [Western Norway](/wiki/Western_Norway "Western Norway") and [Northern Norway](/wiki/Northern_Norway "Northern Norway") attract several hundred thousand foreign tourists each year. In the cities, cultural idiosyncrasies such as the [Holmenkollen ski jump](/wiki/Holmenkollen_ski_jump "Holmenkollen ski jump") in Oslo and [Saga Oseberg](https://no.wikipedia.org/wiki/%C2%ABSaga_Oseberg%C2%BB "no:«Saga Oseberg»") in Tønsberg attract many visitors, as do landmarks such as [Bryggen](/wiki/Bryggen "Bryggen") in Bergen, [Vigeland installation](/wiki/Vigeland_installation "Vigeland installation") in [Frogner Park](/wiki/Frogner_Park "Frogner Park") in Oslo, [Nidaros Cathedral](/wiki/Nidaros_Cathedral "Nidaros Cathedral") in Trondheim, [Fredrikstad Fortress](/wiki/Fredrikstad_Fortress "Fredrikstad Fortress") (*Gamlebyen*) in Fredrikstad, and [the ruin park of Tønsberg Fortress](/wiki/T%C3%B8nsberg_Fortress "Tønsberg Fortress") in Tønsberg.

## Demographics

Main article: [Demographics of Norway](/wiki/Demographics_of_Norway "Demographics of Norway")

### Population

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f6/Annual_population_growth_of_Norway.svg/330px-Annual_population_growth_of_Norway.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Annual_population_growth_of_Norway.svg)

Annual [population growth](/wiki/Population_growth "Population growth") in Norway 1951–2016, in thousands

Norway's population was 5,618,354 people in the third quarter of 2025.[[215]](#cite_note-222) [Norwegians](/wiki/Norwegians "Norwegians") are an ethnic North [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") people. The [total fertility rate](/wiki/Total_fertility_rate "Total fertility rate") (TFR) in 2023 was estimated at 1.40 children born per woman,[[216]](#cite_note-223) below the replacement rate of 2.1, it remains considerably below the high of 4.69 children born per woman in 1877.[[217]](#cite_note-224) In 2024 the [median age](/wiki/Median_age "Median age") of the Norwegian population was 40 years.[[218]](#cite_note-225)

The [Sámi people](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_people "Sámi people") are indigenous to the Far North and have traditionally inhabited central and northern parts of Norway and Sweden, as well as areas in northern Finland and in Russia on the [Kola Peninsula](/wiki/Kola_Peninsula "Kola Peninsula"). Another national minority are the [Kven people](/wiki/Kven_people "Kven people"), descendants of Finnish-speaking people who migrated to northern Norway from the 18th up to the 20th century. From the 19th century up to the 1970s, the Norwegian government tried to assimilate both the Sámi and the Kven, encouraging them to adopt the majority language, culture and religion.[[219]](#cite_note-226) Because of this "[Norwegianization](/wiki/Norwegianization "Norwegianization") process", many families of Sámi or Kven ancestry now identify as ethnic Norwegian.[[220]](#cite_note-227)

The national minorities of Norway are Kvens, [Jews](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Norway "History of the Jews in Norway"), [Forest Finns](/wiki/Forest_Finns "Forest Finns"), and [Romani people](/wiki/Romani_people_in_Norway "Romani people in Norway").[[221]](#cite_note-228)

In 2017, Norway's population ranked first on the [World Happiness Report](/wiki/World_Happiness_Report "World Happiness Report"), and in 2025, it ranked seventh.[[222]](#cite_note-229)[[223]](#cite_note-230)

### Migration

Main articles: [Norwegian diaspora](/wiki/Norwegian_diaspora "Norwegian diaspora") and [Immigration to Norway](/wiki/Immigration_to_Norway "Immigration to Norway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/ed/Percentage_of_Norwegians_born_to_two_Norwegian_parents.png/330px-Percentage_of_Norwegians_born_to_two_Norwegian_parents.png)](/wiki/File:Percentage_of_Norwegians_born_to_two_Norwegian_parents.png)

Norwegians of two Norwegian parents, either born abroad or in Norway as a percentage proportionally and nationally in Norway as of 2021

Particularly in the 19th century, when economic conditions were difficult in Norway, tens of thousands of people migrated to the United States and Canada, where they could work and buy land in frontier areas. Many went to the Midwest and Pacific Northwest. In 2006, according to the US Census Bureau, almost 4.7 million persons identified as [Norwegian Americans](/wiki/Norwegian_Americans "Norwegian Americans"),[[224]](#cite_note-231) which was larger than the population of ethnic Norwegians in Norway itself.[[225]](#cite_note-232) In the 2021 Canadian census, 466,500 Canadian citizens identified as having [Norwegian ancestry](/wiki/Norwegian_Canadians "Norwegian Canadians").[[226]](#cite_note-233)

In 2024, approximately 931,081 individuals (16.8% of the population) of the population of Norway were immigrants. Of these, 386,559 (41.5%) had a [Western](/wiki/Western_culture "Western culture") background (Europe, USA, Canada, Australia and New Zealand), while 544,521 (58.5%) had a non-Western background (Asia, [Africa](/wiki/African_immigration_to_Norway "African immigration to Norway"), South and Central America). 221,459 individuals (4% of the population) were children of immigrants, born in Norway.[[227]](#cite_note-SSB-2024-234)

The largest groups of immigrants are from [Poland](/wiki/Poles_in_Norway "Poles in Norway"), [Lithuania](/wiki/Lithuania "Lithuania"), [Sweden](/wiki/Sweden "Sweden"), [Syria](/wiki/Syrians_in_Norway "Syrians in Norway"), and [Ukraine](/wiki/Ukraine "Ukraine").[[227]](#cite_note-SSB-2024-234)

Immigrants have settled in all Norwegian municipalities. In 2013, the cities with the highest share of immigrants were [Oslo](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo") (32%) and [Drammen](/wiki/Drammen_(town) "Drammen (town)") (27%).[[228]](#cite_note-235) According to [Reuters](/wiki/Reuters "Reuters"), Oslo is the "fastest growing city in Europe because of increased immigration".[[229]](#cite_note-236) In recent years, immigration has accounted for most of Norway's population growth.[[230]](#cite_note-237)

### Religion

Main article: [Religion in Norway](/wiki/Religion_in_Norway "Religion in Norway")

#### Church of Norway

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/2/23/Wikipedia_8733.JPG/250px-Wikipedia_8733.JPG)](/wiki/File:Wikipedia_8733.JPG)

[Nidaros Cathedral](/wiki/Nidaros_Cathedral "Nidaros Cathedral") in [Trondheim](/wiki/Trondheim "Trondheim")

Norway previously had a state church, the Lutheran [Church of Norway](/wiki/Church_of_Norway "Church of Norway"). The [separation of church and state](/wiki/Separation_of_church_and_state#Norway "Separation of church and state") occurred later in Norway compared to most of Europe, and is still incomplete. In 2012, the Norwegian parliament voted to grant the Church of Norway greater autonomy,[[231]](#cite_note-238) a decision which was confirmed in a constitutional amendment on 21 May 2012.[[232]](#cite_note-239)

Previously, parliamentary officials were required to be members of the Church of Norway, and at least half of all government ministers had to be a member. As a state church, the clergy were viewed as state employees, and the central and regional church administrations were part of the state administration.

On 1 January 2017, Norway made the church independent of the state, but retained the Church of Norway's status as the "people's church". Members of the royal family are also still required to be members of the Church.[[233]](#cite_note-240)[[234]](#cite_note-241) The issue of separation of church and state in Norway has increasingly been subject to public debate. This is seen in the evolution of the public school's subject about Christianity, life stance, and religion. The state's loss in a battle at the [European Court of Human Rights](/wiki/European_Court_of_Human_Rights "European Court of Human Rights") at [Strasbourg](/wiki/Strasbourg "Strasbourg") in 2007 led to a decreased focus on Christianity in the subject. This was partly reverted in 2014.[[235]](#cite_note-242) There is also debate about Christmas church service in public schools with regard to a 2024 law banning preaching in education.[[236]](#cite_note-243)

Most Norwegians are registered at baptism as members of the Church of Norway. Many remain in the church to participate in the community and practises such as [baptism](/wiki/Baptism "Baptism"), [confirmation](/wiki/Confirmation "Confirmation"), marriage, and burial rites. About 61.7% of Norwegians were members of the Church of Norway in 2024. In the same year, approximately 50.4% of all newborns were baptised and about 47.6% of all 15-year-olds were [confirmed](/wiki/Confirmation_(Lutheran_Church) "Confirmation (Lutheran Church)") in the church.[[237]](#cite_note-244)

#### Religious affiliation

Official religious affiliation in Norway (31 December 2019):[[238]](#cite_note-245)[[239]](#cite_note-246)[[240]](#cite_note-247)

1. [Evangelical Lutheran Church of Norway](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Church_of_Norway "Evangelical Lutheran Church of Norway") (68.7%)
2. [Catholic Church](/wiki/Catholic_Church_in_Norway "Catholic Church in Norway") (3.08%)
3. [Pentecostal congregations](/wiki/Pentecostalism_in_Norway "Pentecostalism in Norway") (0.76%)
4. [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy_in_Norway "Eastern Orthodoxy in Norway") and [Oriental Orthodox](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy_in_Norway "Oriental Orthodoxy in Norway") (0.53%)
5. [Evangelical Lutheran Free Church](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Free_Church_of_Norway "Evangelical Lutheran Free Church of Norway") (0.36%)
6. Other Christian denominations (2.21%)
7. [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Norway "Islam in Norway") (3.41%)
8. [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism "Buddhism") (0.40%)
9. [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Norway "Hinduism in Norway") (0.21%)
10. [Secular Humanism](/wiki/Norwegian_Humanist_Association "Norwegian Humanist Association") (1.85%)
11. [Unaffiliated](/wiki/Irreligion_in_Norway "Irreligion in Norway") (18.3%)
12. Other Religion (0.09%)

According to the 2010 Eurobarometer Poll, 22% of Norwegian citizens responded that "they believe there is a God", 44% responded that "they believe there is some sort of spirit or life force" and 29% responded that "they don't believe there is any sort of spirit, God or life force". Five per cent gave no response.[[241]](#cite_note-248) In the early 1990s, studies estimated that between 4.7% and 5.3% of Norwegians attended church on a weekly basis.[[242]](#cite_note-249) This figure has dropped to about 2%.[[243]](#cite_note-250)[[244]](#cite_note-251)

In 2010, 10% of the population was [religiously unaffiliated](/wiki/Irreligion "Irreligion"), while another 9% were members of religious communities outside the Church of Norway.[[245]](#cite_note-Norway1-252) Other Christian denominations total about 4.9%[[245]](#cite_note-Norway1-252) of the population, the largest of which is the [Roman Catholic Church](/wiki/Roman_Catholic_Church "Roman Catholic Church"), with 83,000 members, according to 2009 government statistics.[[246]](#cite_note-Norwayreligion2-253) The *Aftenposten* (Evening Post) in October 2012 reported there were about 115,234 registered Roman Catholics in Norway; the reporter estimated that the total number of people with a Roman Catholic background may be 170,000–200,000 or higher.[[247]](#cite_note-254)

Others include [Pentecostals](/wiki/Pentecostalism "Pentecostalism") (39,600),[[246]](#cite_note-Norwayreligion2-253) the [Evangelical Lutheran Free Church of Norway](/wiki/Evangelical_Lutheran_Free_Church_of_Norway "Evangelical Lutheran Free Church of Norway") (19,600),[[246]](#cite_note-Norwayreligion2-253) the [United Methodist Church in Norway](/wiki/United_Methodist_Church_in_Norway "United Methodist Church in Norway") (11,000),[[246]](#cite_note-Norwayreligion2-253) [Baptists](/wiki/Baptists "Baptists") (9,900),[[246]](#cite_note-Norwayreligion2-253) [Eastern Orthodox](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodox "Eastern Orthodox") (9,900),[[246]](#cite_note-Norwayreligion2-253) [Brunstad Christian Church](/wiki/Brunstad_Christian_Church "Brunstad Christian Church") (6,800),[[246]](#cite_note-Norwayreligion2-253) [Seventh-day Adventists](/wiki/Seventh-day_Adventists "Seventh-day Adventists") (5,100),[[246]](#cite_note-Norwayreligion2-253) [Assyrians](/wiki/Assyrian_people "Assyrian people") of the [ACOE](/wiki/Assyrian_Church_of_the_East "Assyrian Church of the East") and the [Chaldean Catholic Church](/wiki/Chaldean_Catholic_Church "Chaldean Catholic Church"), and others. The Swedish, Finnish and Icelandic Lutheran congregations in Norway have about 27,500 members in total.[[246]](#cite_note-Norwayreligion2-253) Other Christian denominations comprise less than 1% each, including 4,000 members in [the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints](/wiki/The_Church_of_Jesus_Christ_of_Latter-day_Saints "The Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints") and 12,000 [Jehovah's Witnesses](/wiki/Jehovah%27s_Witnesses "Jehovah's Witnesses").[[246]](#cite_note-Norwayreligion2-253)
Among non-Christian religions, [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Norway "Islam in Norway") is the largest, with 166,861 registered members (2018), and probably fewer than 200,000 in total.[[248]](#cite_note-255)

Other religions comprise less than 1% each, including 819 adherents of [Judaism](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Norway "History of the Jews in Norway").[[249]](#cite_note-Norwayreligion-256) Indian immigrants introduced Hinduism to Norway, which in 2011 has slightly more than 5,900 adherents, or 1% of non-Lutheran Norwegians.[[249]](#cite_note-Norwayreligion-256) [Sikhism](/wiki/Sikhism "Sikhism") has approximately 3,000 adherents, with most living in Oslo, which has two [gurdwaras](/wiki/Gurdwara "Gurdwara"). Drammen also has a sizeable population of Sikhs; the largest gurdwara in north Europe was built in [Lier](/wiki/Lier,_Norway "Lier, Norway"). There are eleven Buddhist organisations, grouped under the [Buddhistforbundet](/wiki/Buddhist_Federation_of_Norway "Buddhist Federation of Norway") organisation, with slightly over 14,000 members,[[249]](#cite_note-Norwayreligion-256) which make up 0.2% of the population. The [Baháʼí Faith](/wiki/Bah%C3%A1%CA%BC%C3%AD_Faith "Baháʼí Faith") religion has slightly more than 1,000 adherents.[[249]](#cite_note-Norwayreligion-256) Around 1.7% (84,500) of Norwegians belong to the secular [Norwegian Humanist Association](/wiki/Norwegian_Humanist_Association "Norwegian Humanist Association").

From 2006 to 2011, the fastest-growing religious communities in Norway were [Eastern Orthodox Christianity](/wiki/Eastern_Orthodoxy_in_Norway "Eastern Orthodoxy in Norway") and [Oriental Orthodox Christianity](/wiki/Oriental_Orthodoxy_in_Norway "Oriental Orthodoxy in Norway"), which grew in membership by 80%; however, their share of the total population remains small, at 0.2%. It is associated with the immigration from Eritrea and Ethiopia, and to a lesser extent from [Central](/wiki/Central_Europe "Central Europe") and Eastern European and Middle Eastern countries. Other fast-growing religions were [Roman Catholicism](/wiki/Roman_Catholicism_in_Norway "Roman Catholicism in Norway") (78.7%), [Hinduism](/wiki/Hinduism_in_Norway "Hinduism in Norway") (59.6%), [Islam](/wiki/Islam_in_Norway "Islam in Norway") (48.1%), and [Buddhism](/wiki/Buddhism_in_Norway "Buddhism in Norway") (46.7%).[[250]](#cite_note-257)

#### Indigenous religions

As in other Scandinavian countries, the ancient Norse followed a form of [Germanic paganism](/wiki/Germanic_paganism "Germanic paganism") known as [Norse paganism](/wiki/Norse_paganism "Norse paganism"). By the end of the 11th century, when Norway had been [Christianised](/wiki/Christianization_of_Scandinavia "Christianization of Scandinavia"), the indigenous Norse religion and practises were prohibited. Remnants of the native religion and beliefs of Norway survive today in the form of names, referential names of cities and locations, the days of the week, and everyday language. Modern interest in the old ways has led to a revival of pagan religious practises in the form of *[Åsatru](/wiki/Germanic_Neopaganism "Germanic Neopaganism").* The Norwegian *[Åsatrufellesskapet Bifrost](/wiki/%C3%85satrufellesskapet_Bifrost "Åsatrufellesskapet Bifrost")* formed in 1996; in 2011, the fellowship had about 300 members. *Foreningen Forn Sed* was formed in 1999 and has been recognised by the Norwegian government.

The Sámi minority retained their [shamanistic religion](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_shamanism "Sámi shamanism") well into the 18th century, when most converted to Christianity under the influence of Dano-Norwegian Lutheran [missionaries](/wiki/Missionaries "Missionaries"). Today there is a renewed appreciation for the Sámi traditional way of life, which has led to a revival of *[Noaidevuohta](/w/index.php?title=Noaidevuohta&action=edit&redlink=1 "Noaidevuohta (page does not exist)")*.[[251]](#cite_note-258) Some Norwegian and Sámi celebrities are reported to visit [shamans](/wiki/Shamans "Shamans") for guidance.[[252]](#cite_note-259)[[253]](#cite_note-260)

### Health

Main article: [Health in Norway](/wiki/Health_in_Norway "Health in Norway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/9b/Life_expectancy_in_Norway.svg/250px-Life_expectancy_in_Norway.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Life_expectancy_in_Norway.svg)

Development of life expectancy in Norway

Norway was awarded first place according to the UN's [Human Development Index](/wiki/Human_Development_Index "Human Development Index") (HDI) for 2013.[[254]](#cite_note-261) From the 1900s, improvements in public health occurred as a result of development in several areas such as social and [living conditions](/wiki/Living_condition "Living condition"), changes in disease and medical outbreaks, establishment of the health care system, and emphasis on public health matters. [Vaccination](/wiki/Vaccination "Vaccination") and increased treatment opportunities with antibiotics resulted in great improvements within the Norwegian population. Improved hygiene and better nutrition were factors that contributed to improved health.

The disease pattern in Norway changed from communicable diseases to non-communicable diseases and chronic diseases as [cardiovascular disease](/wiki/Cardiovascular_disease "Cardiovascular disease"). Inequalities and social differences are still present in public health in Norway.[[255]](#cite_note-262)

In 2024 the infant mortality rate was 2.1 per 1,000 live births among children under the age of one. For girls it was 1.7 and for boys 2.4, which is the lowest infant mortality rate for boys ever recorded in Norway.[[256]](#cite_note-263)

### Education

Main article: [Education in Norway](/wiki/Education_in_Norway "Education in Norway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/49/NTNU.jpg/250px-NTNU.jpg)](/wiki/File:NTNU.jpg)

The main building of the [Norwegian University of Science and Technology](/wiki/Norwegian_University_of_Science_and_Technology "Norwegian University of Science and Technology") in [Trondheim](/wiki/Trondheim_(city) "Trondheim (city)")

[Higher education in Norway](/wiki/Higher_education_in_Norway "Higher education in Norway") is offered by a range of seven [universities](/wiki/List_of_universities_in_Norway "List of universities in Norway"), five specialised colleges, 25 [university colleges](/wiki/University_college "University college") as well as a range of private colleges. Education follows the [Bologna Process](/wiki/Bologna_Process "Bologna Process") involving [Bachelor](/wiki/Bachelor%27s_degree "Bachelor's degree") (3 years), [Master](/wiki/Master%27s_degree "Master's degree") (2 years) and PhD (3 years) degrees.[[257]](#cite_note-264) Acceptance is offered after finishing [upper secondary school](/wiki/Education_in_Norway "Education in Norway") with general study competence.

Public education is virtually free for citizens from EU/EEA and Switzerland, but other nationalities need to pay tuition fees.[[258]](#cite_note-265)[[259]](#cite_note-266)[[260]](#cite_note-267) Higher education has historically been free for everyone regardless of nationality, but tuition fees for all students from outside EU/EEA and Switzerland was implemented in 2023.[[261]](#cite_note-268)[[262]](#cite_note-269)

The academic year has two [semesters](/wiki/Academic_term "Academic term"), from August to December and from January to June. The ultimate responsibility for the education lies with the [Norwegian Ministry of Education and Research](/wiki/Norwegian_Ministry_of_Education_and_Research "Norwegian Ministry of Education and Research").

### Languages

Main articles: [Languages of Norway](/wiki/Languages_of_Norway "Languages of Norway") and [Norwegian dialects](/wiki/Norwegian_dialects "Norwegian dialects")

See also: [Norwegian language](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language") and [Sámi languages](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_languages "Sámi languages")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/30/Norske_M%C3%A5lgreiner.png/250px-Norske_M%C3%A5lgreiner.png)](/wiki/File:Norske_M%C3%A5lgreiner.png)

The map shows the division of the Norwegian dialects within the main groups.

Norwegian in its two forms, [Bokmål](/wiki/Bokm%C3%A5l "Bokmål") and [Nynorsk](/wiki/Nynorsk "Nynorsk"), is the main national official language of all of Norway. Sámi, a group which includes three separate languages, is recognised as a minority language on the national level and is a co-official language alongside Norwegian in the Sámi administrative linguistic area (*Forvaltningsområdet for samisk språk*) in Northern Norway.[[2]](#cite_note-LanguageCouncilSami-3) [Kven](/wiki/Kven_language "Kven language") is a minority language and is a co-official language alongside Norwegian in one municipality, also in Northern Norway.[[263]](#cite_note-270)[[264]](#cite_note-271)[[265]](#cite_note-272)

#### Norwegian

Norwegian is a [North Germanic](/wiki/North_Germanic_languages "North Germanic languages") language descended from [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse"). It is the main national language of Norway and is spoken throughout the country. Norwegian is spoken natively by over 5 million people mainly in Norway, but is generally understood throughout [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") and to a lesser degree other [Nordic countries](/wiki/Nordic_countries "Nordic countries"). It has two official written forms, *[Bokmål](/wiki/Bokm%C3%A5l "Bokmål")* and *[Nynorsk](/wiki/Nynorsk "Nynorsk")*. Both are used in public administration, schools, churches, and media. Bokmål is the written language used by a majority of about 85%. Around 95% of the population speak Norwegian as their first or native language, although many speak dialects that may differ significantly from the written languages. Norwegian dialects are mutually intelligible, although listeners with limited exposure to dialects other than their own may struggle with certain phrases and pronunciations.

Norwegian is closely related to and generally mutually intelligible with its neighbour [Scandinavian languages](/wiki/North_Germanic_languages "North Germanic languages"); [Danish](/wiki/Danish_language "Danish language") and [Swedish](/wiki/Swedish_language "Swedish language"), and the three main Scandinavian languages thus form both a [dialect continuum](/wiki/Dialect_continuum "Dialect continuum") and a larger language community with about 25 million speakers. All three languages are commonly employed in communication among inhabitants of the Scandinavian countries. As a result of the co-operation within the [Nordic Council](/wiki/Nordic_Council "Nordic Council"), inhabitants of all Nordic countries always have the right to communicate with Norwegian authorities in Danish or Swedish as equal alternatives to Norwegian.[[266]](#cite_note-273) In the 19th and 20th centuries, the Norwegian language was subject to [strong political and cultural controversies](/wiki/Norwegian_language_conflict "Norwegian language conflict"). This led to the development of Nynorsk in the 19th century and to the formation of alternative spelling standards in the 20th century.

#### Sámi and Kven

Several [Uralic](/wiki/Uralic_languages "Uralic languages") Sámi languages, which are related but not generally mutually intelligible, are traditionally spoken by the Sámi people primarily in Northern Norway and to much lesser extent in some parts of Central Norway. Around 15,000 people have officially registered as Sámi in the Sámi census (*Samemanntallet*), but the number of people of recent Sámi heritage is often estimated at 50,000 people. The number of people who have some knowledge of Northern Sámi, including as a second language, is estimated at 25,000 people, but only a minority are native speakers. The other Sámi languages are heavily endangered and spoken by at most a few hundred people. Most people of Sámi heritage are today native speakers of Norwegian as a result of past assimilation policies.[[267]](#cite_note-274)

Speakers have a right to be educated and to receive communication from the government in their own language in a special *forvaltningsområde* (administrative area) for Sámi languages.[[268]](#cite_note-275)[[269]](#cite_note-276) The [Kven](/wiki/Kven_people "Kven people") minority historically spoke the Uralic [Kven language](/wiki/Kven_language "Kven language") (considered a separate language in Norway, but generally perceived as a Finnish dialect in Finland). Today the majority of ethnic Kven have little or no knowledge of the language.[[270]](#cite_note-277) As Norway has ratified the [European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages](/wiki/European_Charter_for_Regional_or_Minority_Languages "European Charter for Regional or Minority Languages") (ECRML) the Kven language together with [Romani](/wiki/Romani_language "Romani language") and [Scandoromani language](/wiki/Scandoromani_language "Scandoromani language") has become officially recognised minority languages.[[271]](#cite_note-278)[[272]](#cite_note-279)

#### Other languages

Some supporters have also advocated making [Norwegian Sign Language](/wiki/Norwegian_Sign_Language "Norwegian Sign Language") an official language.[[273]](#cite_note-280)[[274]](#cite_note-281)

The primary foreign language taught in Norwegian schools is English, and the majority of the population, especially those born after World War II, is fairly fluent in English. German, French and Spanish are also commonly taught as second or, more often, third languages. Russian, Japanese, Italian, [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin"), and rarely [Chinese (Mandarin)](/wiki/Standard_Mandarin "Standard Mandarin") are offered in some schools, mostly in the cities. Traditionally, English, German and French were considered the main foreign languages in Norway. These languages, for instance, were used on [Norwegian passports](/wiki/Norwegian_passport "Norwegian passport") until the 1990s, and university students have a general right to use these languages when submitting their theses.

90% of Norwegians are fluent in English.[[275]](#cite_note-282)

## Culture

Main article: [Culture of Norway](/wiki/Culture_of_Norway "Culture of Norway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Wilhelmine_Seippel.jpg/250px-Wilhelmine_Seippel.jpg)](/wiki/File:Wilhelmine_Seippel.jpg)

Traditional Norwegian farmer's costumes, known as *folkedrakt*, and modern costumes inspired by those costumes, known as *[bunad](/wiki/Bunad "Bunad")*, are widely used on special occasions.

The Norwegian farm culture continues to play a role in contemporary Norwegian culture. In the 19th century, it inspired a strong [romantic nationalistic](/wiki/Norwegian_romantic_nationalism "Norwegian romantic nationalism") movement, which is still visible in the [Norwegian language](/wiki/Norwegian_language "Norwegian language") and [media](/wiki/Category:Mass_media_in_Norway "Category:Mass media in Norway"). Norwegian culture expanded with nationalist efforts to achieve an independent identity in the areas of literature, art and music. This continues today in the performing arts and as a result of government support for exhibitions, cultural projects and artwork.[[276]](#cite_note-283)

### Cinema

Main article: [Cinema of Norway](/wiki/Cinema_of_Norway "Cinema of Norway")

Norwegian cinema has received international recognition. The documentary film *[Kon-Tiki](/wiki/Kon-Tiki_(1950_film) "Kon-Tiki (1950 film)")* (1950) won an [Academy Award](/wiki/Academy_Awards "Academy Awards"). Another notable film is *[The Pinchcliffe Grand Prix](/wiki/The_Pinchcliffe_Grand_Prix "The Pinchcliffe Grand Prix")*, an animated feature film directed by [Ivo Caprino](/wiki/Ivo_Caprino "Ivo Caprino"). The film was released in 1975 and is the most widely seen Norwegian film of all time.[[277]](#cite_note-284) [Nils Gaup](/wiki/Nils_Gaup "Nils Gaup")'s *[Pathfinder](/wiki/Pathfinder_(1987_film) "Pathfinder (1987 film)")* (1987), the story of the [Sámi](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_people "Sámi people"), was nominated for an Oscar. [Berit Nesheim](/wiki/Berit_Nesheim "Berit Nesheim")'s *[The Other Side of Sunday](/wiki/The_Other_Side_of_Sunday "The Other Side of Sunday")* was nominated for an Oscar in 1997.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/a/ab/The_Monn_Keys_%28cropped%29_-_Egil_Monn-Iversen.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Monn_Keys_(cropped)_-_Egil_Monn-Iversen.jpg)

[Egil Ragnar Monn-Iversen](/wiki/Egil_Monn-Iversen "Egil Monn-Iversen") had so much influence in Norwegian culture that he received the nickname *the Godfather*.

Since the 1990s, the film industry has expanded, producing up to 20 feature films each year. Particular successes were *[Kristin Lavransdatter](/wiki/Kristin_Lavransdatter "Kristin Lavransdatter")*, based on a novel by a Nobel Prize winner; *[The Telegraphist](/wiki/The_Telegraphist "The Telegraphist")* and *[Gurin with the Foxtail](/wiki/Gurin_with_the_Foxtail "Gurin with the Foxtail")*. [Knut Erik Jensen](/wiki/Knut_Erik_Jensen "Knut Erik Jensen") was among the more successful new directors, together with [Erik Skjoldbjærg](/wiki/Erik_Skjoldbj%C3%A6rg "Erik Skjoldbjærg"), who is remembered for *[Insomnia](/wiki/Insomnia_(1997_film) "Insomnia (1997 film)")*.[[278]](#cite_note-285) *[Elling](/wiki/Elling "Elling")* and the 2012 adaption of [*Kon-Tiki*](/wiki/Kon-Tiki_(2012_film) "Kon-Tiki (2012 film)") was nominated for an Oscar for the best foreign language film. The TV-series [*Skam*](/wiki/Skam_(TV_series) "Skam (TV series)") created by [Julie Andem](/wiki/Julie_Andem "Julie Andem") received a cult following and international recognition, with many countries making their own adaptations.

Norwegian directors such as [Joachim Rønning](/wiki/Joachim_R%C3%B8nning "Joachim Rønning"), [Anja Breien](/wiki/Anja_Breien "Anja Breien"), [Espen Sandberg](/wiki/Espen_Sandberg "Espen Sandberg"), [Liv Ullmann](/wiki/Liv_Ullmann "Liv Ullmann") and [Morten Tyldum](/wiki/Morten_Tyldum "Morten Tyldum") have made internationally successful movies such as *[The Imitation Game](/wiki/The_Imitation_Game "The Imitation Game")*, [*Passengers*](/wiki/Passengers_(2016_film) "Passengers (2016 film)"), [*Pirates of the Caribbean: Salazar's Revenge*](/wiki/Pirates_of_the_Caribbean:_Dead_Men_Tell_No_Tales "Pirates of the Caribbean: Dead Men Tell No Tales") and *[Maleficent: Mistress of Evil](/wiki/Maleficent:_Mistress_of_Evil "Maleficent: Mistress of Evil")*, as well as the TV series [*Jack Ryan*](/wiki/Jack_Ryan_(TV_series) "Jack Ryan (TV series)") and [*Marco Polo*](/wiki/Marco_Polo_(2014_TV_series) "Marco Polo (2014 TV series)"). Composers include [Thomas Bergersen](/wiki/Thomas_Bergersen "Thomas Bergersen"), who composed for [*Avatar*](/wiki/Avatar_(2009_film) "Avatar (2009 film)"), *[The Dark Knight](/wiki/The_Dark_Knight "The Dark Knight")*, [*Harry Potter*](/wiki/Harry_Potter_(film_series) "Harry Potter (film series)") and [*Narnia*](/wiki/The_Chronicles_of_Narnia_(film_series) "The Chronicles of Narnia (film series)"). [Egil Monn-Iversen](/wiki/Egil_Monn-Iversen "Egil Monn-Iversen") has been one of the most influential modern composers in Norway, having composed scores to over 100 Norwegian movies and TV series.

Norway has been used as filming location for Hollywood and other international productions, including [Star Wars](/wiki/Star_Wars "Star Wars") *[The Empire Strikes Back](/wiki/The_Empire_Strikes_Back "The Empire Strikes Back")* (1980). Among the thousands of movies filmed in Norway include *[Die Another Day](/wiki/Die_Another_Day "Die Another Day")*, *[No Time to Die](/wiki/No_Time_to_Die "No Time to Die")*, *[The Golden Compass](/wiki/The_Golden_Compass_(film) "The Golden Compass (film)")*, *[Spies Like Us](/wiki/Spies_Like_Us "Spies Like Us")*, *[Mission: Impossible – Fallout](/wiki/Mission:_Impossible_%E2%80%93_Fallout "Mission: Impossible – Fallout")* and *[Mission: Impossible – Dead Reckoning Part One](/wiki/Mission:_Impossible_%E2%80%93_Dead_Reckoning_Part_One "Mission: Impossible – Dead Reckoning Part One")*, [*Black Widow*](/wiki/Black_Widow_(2021_film) "Black Widow (2021 film)"), *[Tenet](/wiki/Tenet "Tenet")*, *[Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince](/wiki/Harry_Potter_and_the_Half-Blood_Prince_(film) "Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Prince (film)")* and *[Heroes of Telemark](/wiki/The_Heroes_of_Telemark "The Heroes of Telemark"),* as well as the TV series *[Lilyhammer](/wiki/Lilyhammer "Lilyhammer")* and *[Vikings](/wiki/Vikings_(2013_TV_series) "Vikings (2013 TV series)")*.[[279]](#cite_note-286)

### Music

Main article: [Music of Norway](/wiki/Music_of_Norway "Music of Norway")

See also: [Norwegian music industry](/wiki/Nordic_popular_music#Norway "Nordic popular music")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c0/Edvard_Grieg_%281888%29_by_Elliot_and_Fry_-_02.jpg/250px-Edvard_Grieg_%281888%29_by_Elliot_and_Fry_-_02.jpg)](/wiki/File:Edvard_Grieg_(1888)_by_Elliot_and_Fry_-_02.jpg)

[Edvard Grieg](/wiki/Edvard_Grieg "Edvard Grieg"), composer and pianist

The classical music of the [romantic](/wiki/Romanticism "Romanticism") composers [Edvard Grieg](/wiki/Edvard_Grieg "Edvard Grieg"), [Rikard Nordraak](/wiki/Rikard_Nordraak "Rikard Nordraak") and [Johan Svendsen](/wiki/Johan_Svendsen "Johan Svendsen") is internationally known, as is the modern music of [Arne Nordheim](/wiki/Arne_Nordheim "Arne Nordheim"). Norway's classical performers include [Leif Ove Andsnes](/wiki/Leif_Ove_Andsnes "Leif Ove Andsnes"), a pianist; [Truls Mørk](/wiki/Truls_M%C3%B8rk "Truls Mørk"), an outstanding [cellist](/wiki/Cellist "Cellist"); and the [Wagnerian](/wiki/Wagnerian "Wagnerian") soprano [Kirsten Flagstad](/wiki/Kirsten_Flagstad "Kirsten Flagstad").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

The [Norwegian ballad tradition](/wiki/Scandinavian_ballad_tradition "Scandinavian ballad tradition"), known as the "ballad wave" (Norwegian: *visebølgen*), started as a cultural movement in the 1960s, greatly inspired by the Swedish ballad tradition and its modern representatives such as [Olle Adolphson](/wiki/Olle_Adolphson "Olle Adolphson") and [Cornelis Vreeswijk](/wiki/Cornelis_Vreeswijk "Cornelis Vreeswijk"). Some of its prominent representatives are [Ole Paus](/wiki/Ole_Paus "Ole Paus"), [Lillebjørn Nilsen](/wiki/Lillebj%C3%B8rn_Nilsen "Lillebjørn Nilsen") and [Finn Kalvik](/wiki/Finn_Kalvik "Finn Kalvik").[[280]](#cite_note-287)

The jazz scene is thriving. [Jan Garbarek](/wiki/Jan_Garbarek "Jan Garbarek"), [Terje Rypdal](/wiki/Terje_Rypdal "Terje Rypdal"), [Mari Boine](/wiki/Mari_Boine "Mari Boine"), [Arild Andersen](/wiki/Arild_Andersen "Arild Andersen") and [Bugge Wesseltoft](/wiki/Bugge_Wesseltoft "Bugge Wesseltoft") are internationally recognised while [Paal Nilssen-Love](/wiki/Paal_Nilssen-Love "Paal Nilssen-Love"), [Supersilent](/wiki/Supersilent "Supersilent"), [Jaga Jazzist](/wiki/Jaga_Jazzist "Jaga Jazzist") and [Wibutee](/wiki/Wibutee "Wibutee") are becoming world-class artists.[[281]](#cite_note-288)

Norway has a strong [folk music](/wiki/Folk_music "Folk music") tradition which remains popular.[[282]](#cite_note-289) Among the most prominent folk musicians are [Hardanger fiddlers](/wiki/Hardanger_fiddle "Hardanger fiddle") [Andrea Een](/wiki/Andrea_Een "Andrea Een"), [Olav Jørgen Hegge](/wiki/Olav_J%C3%B8rgen_Hegge "Olav Jørgen Hegge") and [Annbjørg Lien](/wiki/Annbj%C3%B8rg_Lien "Annbjørg Lien"), and the vocalists [Agnes Buen Garnås](/wiki/Agnes_Buen_Garn%C3%A5s "Agnes Buen Garnås"), [Kirsten Bråten Berg](/wiki/Kirsten_Br%C3%A5ten_Berg "Kirsten Bråten Berg") and [Odd Nordstoga](/wiki/Odd_Nordstoga "Odd Nordstoga").

[Norwegian black metal](/wiki/Early_Norwegian_black_metal_scene "Early Norwegian black metal scene"), a form of [rock music in Norway](/wiki/Rock_music_in_Norway "Rock music in Norway"), has been an influence in world music since the late 20th century.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*] Since the 1990s, Norway's export of [black metal](/wiki/Black_metal "Black metal") has been developed by such bands as [Emperor](/wiki/Emperor_(Norwegian_band) "Emperor (Norwegian band)"), [Darkthrone](/wiki/Darkthrone "Darkthrone"), [Gorgoroth](/wiki/Gorgoroth "Gorgoroth"), [Mayhem](/wiki/Mayhem_(band) "Mayhem (band)"), [Burzum](/wiki/Burzum "Burzum") and [Immortal](/wiki/Immortal_(band) "Immortal (band)"). Bands such as [Enslaved](/wiki/Enslaved_(band) "Enslaved (band)"), [Kvelertak](/wiki/Kvelertak "Kvelertak"), [Dimmu Borgir](/wiki/Dimmu_Borgir "Dimmu Borgir") and [Satyricon](/wiki/Satyricon_(band) "Satyricon (band)") have evolved the genre while still garnering worldwide fans.

Notable female solo artists from Norway include [Susanne Sundfør](/wiki/Susanne_Sundf%C3%B8r "Susanne Sundfør"), [Sigrid](/wiki/Sigrid_(singer) "Sigrid (singer)"), [Astrid S](/wiki/Astrid_S "Astrid S"), [Adelén](/wiki/Adel%C3%A9n "Adelén"), [Julie Bergan](/wiki/Julie_Bergan "Julie Bergan"), [Maria Mena](/wiki/Maria_Mena "Maria Mena"), [Tone Damli](/wiki/Tone_Damli "Tone Damli"), [Margaret Berger](/wiki/Margaret_Berger "Margaret Berger"), [Lene Marlin](/wiki/Lene_Marlin "Lene Marlin"), [Christel Alsos](/wiki/Christel_Alsos "Christel Alsos"), [Maria Arredondo](/wiki/Maria_Arredondo "Maria Arredondo"), [Marion Raven](/wiki/Marion_Raven "Marion Raven") and [Marit Larsen](/wiki/Marit_Larsen "Marit Larsen") (both former members of the defunct pop-rock band [M2M](/wiki/M2M_(band) "M2M (band)")), [Lene Nystrøm](/wiki/Lene_Nystr%C3%B8m "Lene Nystrøm") (vocalist of the Danish Eurodance group [Aqua](/wiki/Aqua_(band) "Aqua (band)")), [Anni-Frid Lyngstad](/wiki/Anni-Frid_Lyngstad "Anni-Frid Lyngstad") (vocalist of the Swedish pop group [ABBA](/wiki/ABBA "ABBA")), and [Aurora Aksnes](/wiki/Aurora_(singer) "Aurora (singer)"). Norwegian songwriters and producers for international artists include [Stargate](/wiki/Stargate_(production_team) "Stargate (production team)"), [Espen Lind](/wiki/Espen_Lind "Espen Lind"), Lene Marlin and [Ina Wroldsen](/wiki/Ina_Wroldsen "Ina Wroldsen").

Norway has been a constant competitor in the [Eurovision Song Contest](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest "Eurovision Song Contest"), participating 62 times. Since its first participation in 1960, Norway has won the competition three times: [Bobbysocks](/wiki/Bobbysocks "Bobbysocks")'s win in [1985](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_1985 "Eurovision Song Contest 1985"), [Secret Garden](/wiki/Secret_Garden_(duo) "Secret Garden (duo)")'s win in [1995](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_1995 "Eurovision Song Contest 1995") and [Alexander Rybak](/wiki/Alexander_Rybak "Alexander Rybak")'s win in [2009](/wiki/Eurovision_Song_Contest_2009 "Eurovision Song Contest 2009").[[283]](#cite_note-290) Alexander Rybak's win in 2009 with his song [Fairytale](/wiki/Fairytale_(Alexander_Rybak_song) "Fairytale (Alexander Rybak song)") was a major win in Eurovision's history as it scored the biggest margin of victory ever.[[284]](#cite_note-291) The song was an international hit, peaking at number one in several countries.[[285]](#cite_note-292)

Norway enjoys many music festivals throughout the year, all over the country. Norway is the host of one of the world's biggest [extreme sport](/wiki/Extreme_sport "Extreme sport") festivals with music, [Ekstremsportveko](/wiki/Ekstremsportveko "Ekstremsportveko")—a festival held annually in [Voss Municipality](/wiki/Voss_Municipality "Voss Municipality").
