Oslo is the host of many festivals, such as [Øyafestivalen](/wiki/%C3%98yafestivalen "Øyafestivalen") and [by:Larm](/wiki/By:Larm "By:Larm"). Oslo used to have a summer parade similar to the German [Love Parade](/wiki/Love_Parade "Love Parade"). In 1992, the city of Oslo wanted to adopt the French music festival *Fête de la Musique*. [Fredrik Carl Størmer](/wiki/Fredrik_Carl_St%C3%B8rmer "Fredrik Carl Størmer") established the festival. From its first year, "Musikkens Dag" gathered thousands of people and artists in the streets of Oslo. "Musikkens Dag" is now renamed *Musikkfest Oslo*.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Literature

Main article: [Norwegian literature](/wiki/Norwegian_literature "Norwegian literature")

See also: [List of Norwegian writers](/wiki/List_of_Norwegian_writers "List of Norwegian writers")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/a/a6/Henrik_Ibsen_av_Eilif_Peterssen_1895.jpg/250px-Henrik_Ibsen_av_Eilif_Peterssen_1895.jpg)](/wiki/File:Henrik_Ibsen_av_Eilif_Peterssen_1895.jpg)

[Henrik Ibsen](/wiki/Henrik_Ibsen "Henrik Ibsen"), the most frequently performed dramatist in the world after [Shakespeare](/wiki/William_Shakespeare "William Shakespeare").

The history of Norwegian literature starts with the [pagan](/wiki/Norse_paganism "Norse paganism") [Eddaic poems](/wiki/Poetic_Edda "Poetic Edda") and [skaldic](/wiki/Skald "Skald") verse of the ninth and tenth centuries, with poets such as [Bragi Boddason](/wiki/Bragi_Boddason "Bragi Boddason") and [Eyvindr skáldaspillir](/wiki/Eyvindr_sk%C3%A1ldaspillir "Eyvindr skáldaspillir"). The arrival of Christianity around the year 1000 brought Norway into contact with European medieval learning, [hagiography](/wiki/Hagiography "Hagiography") and history writing. Merged with native oral tradition and Icelandic influence, this influenced the literature written in the late 12th and early 13th centuries. Major works of that period include *[Historia Norwegiæ](/wiki/Historia_Norwegi%C3%A6 "Historia Norwegiæ")*, *[Þiðrekssaga](/wiki/%C3%9Ei%C3%B0rekssaga "Þiðrekssaga")* and *[Konungs skuggsjá](/wiki/Konungs_skuggsj%C3%A1 "Konungs skuggsjá")*.

Little Norwegian literature came out of the period of the Scandinavian Union and the subsequent Dano-Norwegian union (1387–1814), with some notable exceptions such as [Petter Dass](/wiki/Petter_Dass "Petter Dass") and [Ludvig Holberg](/wiki/Ludvig_Holberg "Ludvig Holberg"). During the union with Denmark, the government imposed using only written Danish, which decreased the writing of Norwegian literature.

Two major events precipitated a major resurgence in Norwegian literature: in 1811 a Norwegian university was established in [Christiania](/wiki/Oslo "Oslo"), and in 1814 the Norwegians created their first [Constitution](/wiki/Constitution_of_Norway "Constitution of Norway"). Authors were inspired and became recognised first in Scandinavia, and then worldwide; among them were [Henrik Wergeland](/wiki/Henrik_Wergeland "Henrik Wergeland"), [Peter Christen Asbjørnsen](/wiki/Peter_Christen_Asbj%C3%B8rnsen "Peter Christen Asbjørnsen"), [Jørgen Moe](/wiki/J%C3%B8rgen_Moe "Jørgen Moe") and [Camilla Collett](/wiki/Camilla_Collett "Camilla Collett").

By the late 19th century, in the [Golden Age](/wiki/Golden_Age "Golden Age") of Norwegian literature, the so-called "Great Four" emerged: [Henrik Ibsen](/wiki/Henrik_Ibsen "Henrik Ibsen"), [Bjørnstjerne Bjørnson](/wiki/Bj%C3%B8rnstjerne_Bj%C3%B8rnson "Bjørnstjerne Bjørnson"), [Alexander Kielland](/wiki/Alexander_Kielland "Alexander Kielland"), and [Jonas Lie](/wiki/Jonas_Lie_(writer) "Jonas Lie (writer)"). Bjørnson's "peasant novels", such as *Ein glad gut* (A Happy Boy) and *Synnøve Solbakken*, are typical of the [Norwegian romantic nationalism](/wiki/Norwegian_romantic_nationalism "Norwegian romantic nationalism") of their day. Kielland's novels and short stories are mostly naturalistic. Although an important contributor to early romantic nationalism, (especially *[Peer Gynt](/wiki/Peer_Gynt "Peer Gynt")*), Henrik Ibsen is better known for his pioneering realistic dramas such as *[The Wild Duck](/wiki/The_Wild_Duck "The Wild Duck")* and *[A Doll's House](/wiki/A_Doll%27s_House "A Doll's House").*

In the 20th century, three Norwegian novelists were awarded the [Nobel Prize in Literature](/wiki/Nobel_Prize_in_Literature "Nobel Prize in Literature"): [Bjørnstjerne Bjørnson](/wiki/Bj%C3%B8rnstjerne_Bj%C3%B8rnson "Bjørnstjerne Bjørnson") in 1903, [Knut Hamsun](/wiki/Knut_Hamsun "Knut Hamsun") for the book *Markens grøde* ("[Growth of the Soil](/wiki/Growth_of_the_Soil "Growth of the Soil")") in 1920, and [Sigrid Undset](/wiki/Sigrid_Undset "Sigrid Undset") (known for *[Kristin Lavransdatter](/wiki/Kristin_Lavransdatter "Kristin Lavransdatter")*) in 1928.

### Architecture

Main article: [Architecture of Norway](/wiki/Architecture_of_Norway "Architecture of Norway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/bc/Stave_church_Urnes_-_Panorama_HDR_cropped.jpg/250px-Stave_church_Urnes_-_Panorama_HDR_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Stave_church_Urnes_-_Panorama_HDR_cropped.jpg)

The [Urnes Stave Church](/wiki/Urnes_Stave_Church "Urnes Stave Church") has been listed by [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO") as a [World Heritage Site](/wiki/World_Heritage_Site "World Heritage Site").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Dalen_Hotell_IMG_4773.jpg/250px-Dalen_Hotell_IMG_4773.jpg)](/wiki/File:Dalen_Hotell_IMG_4773.jpg)

[Dalen Hotel](/wiki/Dalen_Hotel "Dalen Hotel") in [Telemark](/wiki/Telemark "Telemark") built in [Dragon Style](/wiki/Dragestil "Dragestil"), a style of design architecture that originated during the [Norwegian romantic nationalism](/wiki/Norwegian_romantic_nationalism "Norwegian romantic nationalism").

With expansive forests, Norway has long had a tradition of building in wood. Many of today's most interesting new buildings are made of wood, reflecting the strong appeal that this material continues to hold for Norwegian designers and builders.[[286]](#cite_note-293)

With Norway's conversion to Christianity, churches were built. Stonework architecture was introduced from Europe for the most important structures, beginning with the construction of [Nidaros Cathedral](/wiki/Nidaros_Cathedral "Nidaros Cathedral") in [Trondheim](/wiki/Trondheim_(city) "Trondheim (city)"). In the early [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), wooden [stave churches](/wiki/Stave_church "Stave church") were constructed throughout Norway. Some of them have survived; they represent Norway's most unusual contribution to architectural history. [Urnes Stave Church](/wiki/Urnes_Stave_Church "Urnes Stave Church") in inner [Sognefjord](/wiki/Sognefjord "Sognefjord") is on [UNESCO](/wiki/UNESCO "UNESCO")'s [World Heritage List](/wiki/World_Heritage_List "World Heritage List"). Another notable example of wooden architecture is the buildings at [Bryggen](/wiki/Bryggen "Bryggen") Wharf in Bergen, also on the list for World Cultural Heritage sites, consisting of a row of tall, narrow wooden structures along the quayside.

In the 17th century, under the Danish monarchy, cities and villages such as [Kongsberg](/wiki/Kongsberg_(town) "Kongsberg (town)") and [Røros](/wiki/R%C3%B8ros_(town) "Røros (town)") were established. The city Kongsberg had a church built in the Baroque style. Traditional wooden buildings that were constructed in Røros have survived.

After Norway's union with Denmark was dissolved in 1814, Oslo became the capital. The architect [Christian H. Grosch](/wiki/Christian_Heinrich_Grosch "Christian Heinrich Grosch") designed the earliest parts of the [University of Oslo](/wiki/University_of_Oslo "University of Oslo"), the [Oslo Stock Exchange](/wiki/Oslo_Stock_Exchange "Oslo Stock Exchange"), and many other buildings and churches constructed in that early national period.

At the beginning of the 20th century, the city of [Ålesund](/wiki/%C3%85lesund_(town) "Ålesund (town)") was rebuilt in the [Art Nouveau](/wiki/Art_Nouveau "Art Nouveau") style, influenced by styles of France. The 1930s, when functionalism dominated, became a strong period for Norwegian architecture. It is only since the late 20th century that Norwegian architects have achieved international renown. One of the most striking modern buildings in Norway is the [Sámi Parliament](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_Parliament_of_Norway "Sámi Parliament of Norway") in [Kárášjohka](/wiki/Karasjok_(village) "Karasjok (village)"), designed by [Stein Halvorson](/w/index.php?title=Stein_Halvorson&action=edit&redlink=1 "Stein Halvorson (page does not exist)") and [Christian Sundby](/w/index.php?title=Christian_Sundby&action=edit&redlink=1 "Christian Sundby (page does not exist)"). Its debating chamber, in timber, is an abstract version of a *[lavvo](/wiki/Lavvu "Lavvu"),* the traditional tent used by the nomadic [Sámi people](/wiki/S%C3%A1mi_people "Sámi people").[[287]](#cite_note-294)

### Art

Main article: [Norwegian art](/wiki/Norwegian_art "Norwegian art")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f4/The_Scream.jpg/250px-The_Scream.jpg)](/wiki/File:The_Scream.jpg)

[The Scream](/wiki/The_Scream "The Scream") by [Edvard Munch](/wiki/Edvard_Munch "Edvard Munch"), 1893

For an extended period, the Norwegian art scene was dominated by artwork from Germany and Holland as well as by the influence of Copenhagen. It was in the 19th century that a truly Norwegian era began, first with portraits, later with impressive landscapes. [Johan Christian Dahl](/wiki/Johan_Christian_Dahl "Johan Christian Dahl"), originally from the Dresden school, eventually returned to paint the landscapes of western Norway, defining Norwegian painting for the first time."[[288]](#cite_note-295)

Norway's newly found independence from Denmark encouraged painters to develop their Norwegian identity, especially with landscape painting by artists such as [Kitty Kielland](/wiki/Kitty_Lange_Kielland "Kitty Lange Kielland"), a female painter who studied under [Hans Gude](/wiki/Hans_Gude "Hans Gude"), and [Harriet Backer](/wiki/Harriet_Backer "Harriet Backer"), another pioneer among female artists, influenced by [impressionism](/wiki/Impressionism "Impressionism"). [Frits Thaulow](/wiki/Frits_Thaulow "Frits Thaulow"), an impressionist, was influenced by the art scene in Paris as was [Christian Krohg](/wiki/Christian_Krohg "Christian Krohg"), a realist painter, famous for his paintings of prostitutes.[[289]](#cite_note-296)

Of particular note is [Edvard Munch](/wiki/Edvard_Munch "Edvard Munch"), a symbolist/expressionist painter who became world-famous for *[The Scream](/wiki/The_Scream "The Scream")* which is said to represent the anxiety of modern man. Other notable works from Munch includes [The Sick Child](/wiki/The_Sick_Child_(Munch) "The Sick Child (Munch)"), [Madonna](/wiki/Madonna_(Munch) "Madonna (Munch)") and [Puberty](/wiki/Puberty_(Munch) "Puberty (Munch)").

Other artists of note include [Harald Sohlberg](/wiki/Harald_Sohlberg "Harald Sohlberg"), a neo-romantic painter remembered for his paintings of [Røros](/wiki/R%C3%B8ros_(town) "Røros (town)"), and [Odd Nerdrum](/wiki/Odd_Nerdrum "Odd Nerdrum"), a figurative painter who maintains that his work is not art, but [kitsch](/wiki/Kitsch "Kitsch").

### Cuisine

Main article: [Norwegian cuisine](/wiki/Norwegian_cuisine "Norwegian cuisine")

Norway's culinary traditions show the influence of long seafaring and farming traditions, with [salmon](/wiki/Salmon "Salmon") (fresh and cured), [herring](/wiki/Herring "Herring") (pickled or marinated), [trout](/wiki/Trout "Trout"), [codfish](/wiki/Cod "Cod"), and other seafood, balanced by cheeses (such as [brunost](/wiki/Brunost "Brunost"), [Jarlsberg cheese](/wiki/Jarlsberg_cheese "Jarlsberg cheese"), and [gamalost](/wiki/Gamalost "Gamalost")), dairy products, and breads (predominantly dark/darker).

[Lefse](/wiki/Lefse "Lefse") is a Norwegian potato flatbread, usually topped with large amounts of butter and sugar, most commonly eaten around Christmas. Traditional Norwegian dishes include [lutefisk](/wiki/Lutefisk "Lutefisk"), [smalahove](/wiki/Smalahove "Smalahove"), [pinnekjøtt](/wiki/Pinnekj%C3%B8tt "Pinnekjøtt"), [raspeball](/wiki/Raspeball "Raspeball"), and [fårikål](/wiki/F%C3%A5rik%C3%A5l "Fårikål").[[290]](#cite_note-297) A Norwegian speciality is rakefisk, which is fermented trout, consumed with thin flatbread and sour cream. The most popular pastry is vaffel.

### Sports

|  |  |
| --- | --- |

See also: [List of Norwegian sport governing bodies](/wiki/List_of_Norwegian_sport_governing_bodies "List of Norwegian sport governing bodies") and [Football in Norway](/wiki/Football_in_Norway "Football in Norway")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cb/Marit_Bj%C3%B8rgen_Holmenkollen_2011_001.jpg/250px-Marit_Bj%C3%B8rgen_Holmenkollen_2011_001.jpg)](/wiki/File:Marit_Bj%C3%B8rgen_Holmenkollen_2011_001.jpg)

Skier [Marit Bjørgen](/wiki/Marit_Bj%C3%B8rgen "Marit Bjørgen") from Norway is the [most successful Winter Olympian](/wiki/List_of_multiple_Winter_Olympic_medallists "List of multiple Winter Olympic medallists") of all time, with 15 medals

Sports are a central part of Norwegian culture, and popular sports include [cross-country skiing](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_(sport) "Cross-country skiing (sport)"), [ski jumping](/wiki/Ski_jumping "Ski jumping"), [mountaineering](/wiki/Mountaineering "Mountaineering"), [hiking](/wiki/Hiking "Hiking"), association football, [handball](/wiki/Handball "Handball"), [biathlon](/wiki/Biathlon "Biathlon"), [speed skating](/wiki/Long_track_speed_skating "Long track speed skating"), and, to a lesser degree, [ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey").

Norway is known internationally for its role in the development of modern winter sports, particularly skiing. From the 19th century Norway also became a premier [mountaineering](/wiki/Mountaineering "Mountaineering") destination, with books such as [William Cecil Slingsby](/wiki/William_Cecil_Slingsby "William Cecil Slingsby")'s *[Norway, the Northern Playground](/wiki/Norway,_the_Northern_Playground "Norway, the Northern Playground")* contributing to the country's popularity among early mountain climbers.[[291]](#cite_note-298)

Association football is the most popular sport in Norway in terms of active membership. In 2014–2015 polling, football ranked far behind [biathlon](/wiki/Biathlon "Biathlon") and [cross-country skiing](/wiki/Cross-country_skiing_(sport) "Cross-country skiing (sport)") in terms of popularity as spectator sports.[[292]](#cite_note-299) [Ice hockey](/wiki/Ice_hockey "Ice hockey") was the biggest indoor sport as of 2013.[[293]](#cite_note-300) The [women's handball national team](/wiki/Norway_women%27s_national_handball_team "Norway women's national handball team") has won several titles, including two [Summer Olympics](/wiki/Summer_Olympics "Summer Olympics") championships ([2008](/wiki/Handball_at_the_2008_Summer_Olympics "Handball at the 2008 Summer Olympics"), [2012](/wiki/Handball_at_the_2012_Summer_Olympics "Handball at the 2012 Summer Olympics")), three [World Championships](/wiki/World_Women%27s_Handball_Championship "World Women's Handball Championship") ([1999](/wiki/1999_World_Women%27s_Handball_Championship "1999 World Women's Handball Championship"), [2011](/wiki/2011_World_Women%27s_Handball_Championship "2011 World Women's Handball Championship"), [2015](/wiki/2015_World_Women%27s_Handball_Championship "2015 World Women's Handball Championship")), and six [European Championship](/wiki/European_Women%27s_Handball_Championship "European Women's Handball Championship") ([1998](/wiki/1998_European_Women%27s_Handball_Championship "1998 European Women's Handball Championship"), [2004](/wiki/2004_European_Women%27s_Handball_Championship "2004 European Women's Handball Championship"), [2006](/wiki/2006_European_Women%27s_Handball_Championship "2006 European Women's Handball Championship"), [2008](/wiki/2008_European_Women%27s_Handball_Championship "2008 European Women's Handball Championship"), [2010](/wiki/2010_European_Women%27s_Handball_Championship "2010 European Women's Handball Championship"), [2014](/wiki/2014_European_Women%27s_Handball_Championship "2014 European Women's Handball Championship")).[*[needs update](/wiki/Wikipedia:Manual_of_Style/Dates_and_numbers#Chronological_items "Wikipedia:Manual of Style/Dates and numbers")*] Men's handball experienced a boom in the late 2010s and early 2020s, with star players including [Sander Sagosen](/wiki/Sander_Sagosen "Sander Sagosen") and [Magnus Abelvik Rød](/wiki/Magnus_Abelvik_R%C3%B8d "Magnus Abelvik Rød"), the [Norway national handball team](/wiki/Norway_national_handball_team "Norway national handball team") reaching the finals of the [2017](/wiki/2017_World_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "2017 World Men's Handball Championship") and [2019](/wiki/2019_World_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "2019 World Men's Handball Championship") [IHF World Men's Handball Championships](/wiki/IHF_World_Men%27s_Handball_Championship "IHF World Men's Handball Championship"), and [Kolstad Håndball](/wiki/Kolstad_H%C3%A5ndball "Kolstad Håndball") having among the highest group stage average crowds in the [2023–24](/wiki/2023%E2%80%9324_EHF_Champions_League "2023–24 EHF Champions League") and [2024–25](/wiki/2024%E2%80%9325_EHF_Champions_League "2024–25 EHF Champions League") [EHF Champions League](/wiki/EHF_Champions_League "EHF Champions League") seasons.

In association football, the [women's national team](/wiki/Norway_women%27s_national_football_team "Norway women's national football team") has won the [FIFA Women's World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "FIFA Women's World Cup") in [1995](/wiki/1995_FIFA_Women%27s_World_Cup "1995 FIFA Women's World Cup") and the [Olympic Football Tournament](/wiki/Football_at_the_Olympics "Football at the Olympics") in [2000](/wiki/Football_at_the_2000_Summer_Olympics_%E2%80%93_Women%27s_tournament "Football at the 2000 Summer Olympics – Women's tournament"). The women's team also has two [UEFA European Women's Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Women%27s_Championship "UEFA European Women's Championship") titles ([1987](/wiki/1987_European_Competition_for_Women%27s_Football "1987 European Competition for Women's Football"), [1993](/wiki/UEFA_Women%27s_Euro_1993 "UEFA Women's Euro 1993")). The [men's national football team](/wiki/Norway_national_football_team "Norway national football team") has participated three times in the [FIFA World Cup](/wiki/FIFA_World_Cup "FIFA World Cup") ([1938](/wiki/1938_FIFA_World_Cup "1938 FIFA World Cup"), [1994](/wiki/1994_FIFA_World_Cup "1994 FIFA World Cup"), and [1998](/wiki/1998_FIFA_World_Cup "1998 FIFA World Cup")), and once in the [European Championship](/wiki/UEFA_European_Football_Championship "UEFA European Football Championship") ([2000](/wiki/UEFA_Euro_2000 "UEFA Euro 2000")). The highest FIFA ranking Norway has achieved is second, a position it has held twice, in 1993 and in 1995.[[294]](#cite_note-301)

Norwegian players in the [National Football League](/wiki/National_Football_League "National Football League") in [American football](/wiki/American_football "American football") include [Halvor Hagen](/wiki/Halvor_Hagen "Halvor Hagen"), [Bill Irgens](/wiki/Bill_Irgens "Bill Irgens"), [Leif Olve Dolonen Larsen](/wiki/Leif_Olve_Dolonen_Larsen "Leif Olve Dolonen Larsen"), [Mike Mock](/wiki/Mike_Mock "Mike Mock"), and [Jan Stenerud](/wiki/Jan_Stenerud "Jan Stenerud").[[295]](#cite_note-302)

[Bandy](/wiki/Bandy "Bandy") is a traditional sport in Norway and the country is one of the four founders of [Federation of International Bandy](/wiki/Federation_of_International_Bandy "Federation of International Bandy"). As of January 2018, [the men's national team](/wiki/Norway_national_bandy_team "Norway national bandy team") has captured one silver and one bronze, while [the women's national team](/wiki/Norway_women%27s_national_bandy_team "Norway women's national bandy team") has managed five bronzes at [the World Championships](/wiki/Bandy#Overview_of_international_competitions "Bandy").

Norway first participated at the [Olympic Games](/wiki/Olympic_Games "Olympic Games") in 1900, and has sent athletes to compete in every Games since then, except for the sparsely attended [1904 Games](/wiki/1904_Summer_Olympics "1904 Summer Olympics") and the [1980 Summer Olympics](/wiki/1980_Summer_Olympics "1980 Summer Olympics") in Moscow when they participated in the [American-led boycott](/wiki/1980_Summer_Olympics_boycott "1980 Summer Olympics boycott"). Norway leads [the overall medal tables](/wiki/All-time_Olympic_Games_medal_table "All-time Olympic Games medal table") at the [Winter Olympic Games](/wiki/Winter_Olympic_Games "Winter Olympic Games") by a considerable margin. Norway has hosted the Games on two occasions:

* [1952 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1952_Winter_Olympics "1952 Winter Olympics") in Oslo
* [1994 Winter Olympics](/wiki/1994_Winter_Olympics "1994 Winter Olympics") in [Lillehammer](/wiki/Lillehammer "Lillehammer")

It also hosted the [2016 Winter Youth Olympics](/wiki/2016_Winter_Youth_Olympics "2016 Winter Youth Olympics") in Lillehammer, making Norway the first country to host both Winter regular and Youth Olympics.

Norway featured a women's national team in [beach volleyball](/wiki/Beach_volleyball "Beach volleyball") that competed at the [2018–2020 CEV Beach Volleyball Continental Cup](/wiki/2018%E2%80%932020_CEV_Beach_Volleyball_Continental_Cup "2018–2020 CEV Beach Volleyball Continental Cup"),[[296]](#cite_note-303) and later a men's national team in [beach volleyball at the 2020 Summer Olympics](/wiki/Beach_volleyball_at_the_2020_Summer_Olympics "Beach volleyball at the 2020 Summer Olympics") that won gold metal.

[Chess](/wiki/Chess "Chess") has gained huge popularity in Norway. [Magnus Carlsen](/wiki/Magnus_Carlsen "Magnus Carlsen"), a Norwegian, was the [world chess champion](/wiki/World_Chess_Championship "World Chess Championship") between 2013 and 2023.[[297]](#cite_note-304) Many of the biggest chess tournaments are broadcast live on national television, with the [World Rapid](/wiki/World_Rapid_Chess_Championship "World Rapid Chess Championship") and [Blitz Chess Championships](/wiki/World_Blitz_Chess_Championship "World Blitz Chess Championship") airing on the country's biggest television channel [NRK1](/wiki/NRK1 "NRK1").[[298]](#cite_note-305)

Norway has produced many [track and field](/wiki/Track_and_field "Track and field") athletes at the highest international levels, including, but not limited to, [Egil Danielsen](/wiki/Egil_Danielsen "Egil Danielsen") and [Andreas Thorkildsen](/wiki/Andreas_Thorkildsen "Andreas Thorkildsen") (Men's [javelin throw](/wiki/Javelin_throw "Javelin throw")), [Grete Waitz](/wiki/Grete_Waitz "Grete Waitz") (Women's long-distance running), [Vebjørn Rodal](/wiki/Vebj%C3%B8rn_Rodal "Vebjørn Rodal"), [Jakob Ingebrigtsen](/wiki/Jakob_Ingebrigtsen "Jakob Ingebrigtsen"), and [Henrik Ingebrigtsen](/wiki/Henrik_Ingebrigtsen "Henrik Ingebrigtsen") (Men's middle-distance running), [Karsten Warholm](/wiki/Karsten_Warholm "Karsten Warholm") (Men's 400m running and hurdles), [Sander Skotheim](/wiki/Sander_Skotheim "Sander Skotheim") and [Markus Rooth](/wiki/Markus_Rooth "Markus Rooth") (Men's [pentathlon](/wiki/Pentathlon "Pentathlon")), and [Kristian Blummenfelt](/wiki/Kristian_Blummenfelt "Kristian Blummenfelt") (Men's [triathlon](/wiki/Triathlon "Triathlon")).
